Abstract Malware, also known as malicious code, refers to a program that is covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim’s data, applications, or operating system. Malware is the most common external threat to most hosts, causing widespread damage and disruption and necessitating extensive recovery efforts within most organizations. This publication provides recommendations for improving an organization’s malware incident prevention measures. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones. Keywords incident response; information security; malware Acknowledgments The authors, Murugiah Souppaya of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and Karen Scarfone of Scarfone Cybersecurity, thank their colleagues who assisted with developing this revision of the publication. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones.","searchType":"azoresBingUnscopedSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"url":"https://www.eccouncil.org/cybersecurity-exchange/incident-handling/what-is-incident-response-life-cycle/","rankScore":1,"snippet":"NIST Incident Response Life Cycle in Cybersecurity | EC-Council Understanding the Incident Response Life Cycle March 30, 2022 | Incident Handling Incident response management is an integral part of cybersecurity operations. Incident responders are the first to react to any security incident: They help organizations identify, contain, eradicate, and recover from the incident. Incident handlers help create incident management plans for detection and recovery procedures. Incident handlers—and the entire company—can use these plans in the event of a cyberattack. This article will cover what you need to know about the incident response life cycle and how to help businesses prevent, or manage the aftermath of, a cyberattack. What Is the Incident Response Life Cycle? The incident response life cycle is a series of procedures executed in the event of a security incident. These steps define the workflow for the overall incident response process. Each stage entails a specific set of actions that an organization should complete. The Five Phases of the Incident Response Life Cycle There are several ways to define the incident response life cycle. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST; Cichonski et al., 2012) developed a framework for incident handling, which is the most commonly used model. The process outlined in the NIST framework includes five phases: Preparation Detection and analysis Containment Eradication and recovery Post-event activity 1. Preparation In this phase, the business creates an incident management plan that can detect an incident in the organization’s environment. The preparation step involves, for example, identifying different malware attacks and determining what their impact on systems would be. It also involves ensuring that an organization has the tools to respond to an incident and the appropriate security measures in place to stop an incident from happening in the first place. 2. Detection and Analysis An incident response analyst is responsible for collecting and analyzing data to find any clues to help identify the source of an attack. In this step, analysts identify the nature of the attack and its impact on systems. The business and the security professionals it works with utilize the tools and indicators of compromise (IOCs) that have been developed to track the attacked systems. 3. Containment, Eradication, and Recovery This is the main phase of security incident response, in which the responders take action to stop any further damage. This phase encompasses three steps: Containment. In this step, all possible methods are used to prevent the spread of malware or viruses. Actions might include disconnecting systems from networks, quarantining infected systems (Landesman, 2021), or blocking traffic to and from known malicious IP addresses. Eradication. After containing the security issue in question, the malicious code or software needs to be eradicated from the environment. This might involve using antivirus tools or manual removal techniques (Williams, 2022). It will also include ensuring that all security software is up to date in order to prevent any future incidents. Recovery. After eliminating the malware, restoring all systems to their pre-incident state is essential (Mazzoli, 2021). This might involve restoring data from backups, rebuilding infected systems, and re-enabling disabled accounts. Explore Incident Response With ECIH Certification Post-Event Activity The final phase of the incident response life cycle is to perform a postmortem of the entire incident (Cynet, 2022). This helps the organization understand how the incident took place and what it can do to prevent such incidents from happening in the future. The lessons learned during this phase can improve the organization’s incident security protocols and make its security strategy more robust and effective. How to Become Ultimate Cybersecurity Incident Handler with ECIH - YouTube How to Become Ultimate Cybersecurity Incident Handler with ECIH EC-Council Watch on Tips for Improving an Incident Response Plan There are many ways to improve an organization’s incident management plan (HIMSS, 2022). Identify and train incident handlers in case there is a security breach. Ensure that all employees know their responsibilities when such an event occurs. These responsibilities may vary, but they will likely involve when to report an issue, who to contact, and what tools to immediately deploy in the event of a breach. Create effective communication channels across teams, ensuring that each person reports to their assigned contact. This helps ensure quick detection and recovery from any incidents in real time without losing much valuable information or data. Maintain logs for each system and update them regularly, leaving no gaps in the data. The creation of such logs can be useful in identifying the source of a security breach and preventing similar events in the future. Regularly test the incident response plan so that the documentation stays up to date with any changes made to security policies or new technologies introduced to the organization’s infrastructure. Become a Certified Incident Handler Prevent Security Incidents with an Incident Handler Certification At the end of the day, businesses need to ensure that they have the appropriate resources on hand to prevent a security breach from occurring and to know how to handle it if one does. EC-Council’s Certified Incident Handler (ECIH) certification program teaches cybersecurity professionals the skills they need to prepare for such an event and trains them to detect, analyze, and prepare for any security-related incident within an organization. Having ECIH-certified personnel on hand can benefit businesses in numerous ways, including reducing damages, increasing response times to security breaches, and greatly improving security posture. Interested in learning more? Visit the ECIH program page for information on the course, and contact EC-Council today to learn how to get certified. CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health\u0027s primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. Console Login: falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) — Use your Harris Health SSO credentials. If locked out, contact your SOC Manager. What Harris Health Uses CrowdStrike Falcon For \u003Ctable\u003E \u003Cthead\u003E\u003Ctr\u003E\u003Cth\u003EModule\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EPurpose at Harris Health\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EStatus\u003C/th\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E\u003C/thead\u003E \u003Ctbody\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Prevent\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ENext-gen antivirus — blocks known malware, exploits, and ransomware at the endpoint\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Insight XDR\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EEndpoint detection and response — behavioral analytics, process tree visibility, IOC matching\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Overwatch\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003E24/7 human-led threat hunting — proactive alerts from CrowdStrike\u0027s elite threat hunters\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive (Phase 0)\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Intelligence (CAO)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EThreat intel, dark web monitoring for Harris Health, adversary tracking, healthcare threat profiles\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive (Phase 0)\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIOC Management\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECustom indicator blocking/detection — IPs, domains, file hashes pushed from TIP or manually\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EReal-Time Response (RTR)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ERemote shell to endpoints for live forensics, file retrieval, process kill, script execution\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ENetwork Containment\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIsolate a compromised endpoint from the network while maintaining CrowdStrike cloud connectivity\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003C/tbody\u003E \u003C/table\u003E Key Architecture Note: CrowdStrike Falcon is the primary EDR. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside it (for Purview DLP telemetry only, not for active protection). If you see a Defender alert, it\u0027s informational — CrowdStrike is the blocking/containment tool. A 6-9 month parallel evaluation is underway to make a data-driven decision on whether to keep both. \uD83D\uDDA5️ Console Navigation Use this section to find your way around the Falcon console. The console is accessed via falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using Harris Health SSO. Key Areas You\u0027ll Use Daily 1 Activity Dashboard (Home) Where: Landing page after login, or left menu → Activity → Detections What it shows: All active detections across the environment, sorted by severity and time. This is your primary alert queue for CrowdStrike alerts. L1 Action: Start here every shift. Filter by severity (Critical/High first), then review unassigned detections. 2 Host Management Where: Left menu → Host setup and management → Host Management What it shows: All enrolled endpoints — hostname, IP, OS, sensor version, containment status, last seen time. L1 Action: Search for a specific host by hostname or IP. Check if a host is online, contained, or in Reduced Functionality Mode (RFM). This is where you initiate Network Containment . 3 Threat Intelligence → IOC Management Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management What it shows: All custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the TIP automatically or manually by analysts. L1 Action: Add new IOCs from threat advisories, search for existing IOCs, check if an indicator is already blocked. 4 Threat Intelligence → Intelligence Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → Intelligence What it shows: CrowdStrike\u0027s threat intelligence database — adversary profiles, malware families, vulnerability intelligence, IOC reputation lookups.","searchType":"dataverseSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"rankScore":0.0317540317773819,"snippet":". If it shows \u0022Containment Pending,\u0022 the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by \u0022Contained\u0022 status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click \u0022Lift Containment\u0022 Click the \u0022Status: Contained\u0022 button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., \u0022Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345\u0022). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from \u0022Lift Containment Pending\u0022 to \u0022Normal\u0022 . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. \uD83C\uDFAF IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You\u0027ll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click \u0022Add IOC\u0022 Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect \u0026 Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., \u0022Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042\u0022 or \u0022CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345\u0022. 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it\u0027s already in the system before adding a duplicate. \uD83D\uDD0D Hash \u0026 IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it\u0027s malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike\u0027s intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It\u0027s the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70\u002B antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2. \uD83D\uDCBB Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Contact SOC Manager","searchType":"dataverseSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"url":"https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/83/r1/final","rankScore":1,"snippet":"SP 800-83 Rev. 1, Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling for Desktops and Laptops | CSRC Publications NIST SP 800-83 Rev. 1 Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling for Desktops and Laptops Date Published: July 2013 Supersedes: SP 800-83 (11/23/2005) Author (s) Murugiah Souppaya (NIST), Karen Scarfone (Scarfone Cybersecurity) Abstract Malware, also known as malicious code, refers to a program that is covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim’s data, applications, or operating system. Malware is the most common external threat to most hosts, causing widespread damage and disruption and necessitating extensive recovery efforts within most organizations. This publication provides recommendations for improving an organization’s malware incident prevention measures. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones. ITL develops tests, test methods, reference data, proof of concept implementations, and technical analyses to advance the development and productive use of information technology. ITL’s responsibilities include the development of management, administrative, technical, and physical standards and guidelines for the cost-effective security and privacy of other than national security-related information in Federal information systems. The Special Publication 800-series reports on ITL’s research, guidelines, and outreach efforts in information system security, and its collaborative activities with industry, government, and academic organizations. Abstract Malware, also known as malicious code, refers to a program that is covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim’s data, applications, or operating system. Malware is the most common external threat to most hosts, causing widespread damage and disruption and necessitating extensive recovery efforts within most organizations. This publication provides recommendations for improving an organization’s malware incident prevention measures. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones. CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health\u0027s primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. Console Login: falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) — Use your Harris Health SSO credentials. If locked out, contact your SOC Manager. Malware is the most common external threat to most hosts, causing widespread damage and disruption and necessitating extensive recovery efforts within most organizations. This publication provides recommendations for improving an organization’s malware incident prevention measures. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones. If it shows \u0022Containment Pending,\u0022 the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by \u0022Contained\u0022 status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click \u0022Lift Containment\u0022 Click the \u0022Status: Contained\u0022 button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., \u0022Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345\u0022). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from \u0022Lift Containment Pending\u0022 to \u0022Normal\u0022 . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. \uD83C\uDFAF IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You\u0027ll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click \u0022Add IOC\u0022 Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect \u0026 Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., \u0022Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042\u0022 or \u0022CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345\u0022. 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it\u0027s already in the system before adding a duplicate. \uD83D\uDD0D Hash \u0026 IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it\u0027s malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike\u0027s intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It\u0027s the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70\u002B antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2. \uD83D\uDCBB Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Abstract Malware, also known as malicious code, refers to a program that is covertly inserted into another program with the intent to destroy data, run destructive or intrusive programs, or otherwise compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the victim’s data, applications, or operating system. Malware is the most common external threat to most hosts, causing widespread damage and disruption and necessitating extensive recovery efforts within most organizations. This publication provides recommendations for improving an organization’s malware incident prevention measures. It also gives extensive recommendations for enhancing an organization’s existing incident response capability so that it is better prepared to handle malware incidents, particularly widespread ones. CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health\u0027s primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Cybersecurity 101: The Fundamentals of Cybersecurity \u003E Malware Explained \u003E Malware Analysis Explained Malware Analysis Explained Kurt Baker - March 04, 2025 What is malware analysis? Malware analysis is the process of understanding the behavior and purpose of a suspicious file or URL. The output of the analysis aids in the detection and mitigation of the potential threat. The key benefit of malware analysis is that it helps incident responders and security analysts: Pragmatically triage incidents by level of severity Uncover hidden indicators of compromise (IOCs) that should be blocked Improve the efficacy of IOC alerts and notifications Enrich context when threat hunting Types of malware analysis The analysis may be conducted in a manner that is static, dynamic or a hybrid of the two. Static analysis Basic static analysis does not require that the code is actually run. Instead, static analysis examines the file for signs of malicious intent. It can be useful to identify malicious infrastructure, libraries or packed files. Technical indicators are identified such as file names, hashes, strings such as IP addresses, domains, and file header data can be used to determine whether that file is malicious. In addition, tools like disassemblers and network analyzers can be used to observe the malware without actually running it in order to collect information on how the malware works. CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report AI threats have reached a critical turning point. Access the definitive look at the cyber threat landscape. Download However, since static analysis does not actually run the code, sophisticated malware can include malicious runtime behavior that can go undetected. For example, if a file generates a string that then downloads a malicious file based upon the dynamic string, it could go undetected by a basic static analysis. Enterprises have turned to dynamic analysis for a more complete understanding of the behavior of the file. Dynamic analysis Dynamic malware analysis executes suspected malicious code in a safe environment called a sandbox. This closed system enables security professionals to watch the malware in action without the risk of letting it infect their system or escape into the enterprise network. Dynamic analysis provides threat hunters and incident responders with deeper visibility, allowing them to uncover the true nature of a threat. As a secondary benefit, automated sandboxing eliminates the time it would take to reverse engineer a file to discover the malicious code. The challenge with dynamic analysis is that adversaries are smart, and they know sandboxes are out there, so they have become very good at detecting them. To deceive a sandbox, adversaries hide code inside them that may remain dormant until certain conditions are met. Only then does the code run. Hybrid analysis (includes both of the techniques above) Basic static analysis isn’t a reliable way to detect sophisticated malicious code, and sophisticated malware can sometimes hide from the presence of sandbox technology. By combining basic and dynamic analysis techniques, hybrid analysis provide security team the best of both approaches – primarily because it can detect malicious code that is trying to hide, and then can extract many more indicators of compromise (IOCs) by statically and previously unseen code. Hybrid analysis helps detect unknown threats, even those from the most sophisticated malware. For example, one of the things hybrid analysis does is apply static analysis to data generated by behavioral analysis – like when a piece of malicious code runs and generates some changes in memory. Dynamic analysis would detect that, and analysts would be alerted to circle back and perform basic static analysis on that memory dump. As a result, more IOCs would be generated and zero-day exploits would be exposed. Learn More Falcon Sandbox enables cybersecurity teams of all skill levels to increase their understanding of the threats they face and use that knowledge to defend against future attacks. Learn more about Falcon Sandbox here. Malware analysis use cases Malware detection Adversaries are employing more sophisticated techniques to avoid traditional detection mechanisms. By providing deep behavioral analysis and by identifying shared code, malicious functionality or infrastructure, threats can be more effectively detected. In addition, an output of malware analysis is the extraction of IOCs. The IOCs may then be fed into SEIMs, threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) and security orchestration tools to aid in alerting teams to related threats in the future. Threat alerts and triage Malware analysis solutions provide higher-fidelity alerts earlier in the attack life cycle. Therefore, teams can save time by prioritizing the results of these alerts over other technologies. Incident response The goal of the incident response (IR) team is to provide root cause analysis, determine impact and succeed in remediation and recovery. The malware analysis process aids in the efficiency and effectiveness of this effort. Threat hunting Malware analysis can expose behavior and artifacts that threat hunters can use to find similar activity, such as access to a particular network connection, port or domain. By searching firewall and proxy logs or SIEM data, teams can use this data to find similar threats. Malware research Academic or industry malware researchers perform malware analysis to gain an understanding of the latest techniques, exploits and tools used by adversaries. Stages of malware analysis Static properties analysis Static properties include strings embedded in the malware code, header details, hashes, metadata, embedded resources, etc. This type of data may be all that is needed to create IOCs, and they can be acquired very quickly because there is no need to run the program in order to see them. Insights gathered during the static properties analysis can indicate whether a deeper investigation using more comprehensive techniques is necessary and determine which steps should be taken next. Interactive behavior analysis Behavioral analysis is used to observe and interact with a malware sample running in a lab. Analysts seek to understand the sample’s registry, file system, process and network activities. They may also conduct memory forensics to learn how the malware uses memory. If the analysts suspect that the malware has a certain capability, they can set up a simulation to test their theory. Behavioral analysis requires a creative analyst with advanced skills. The process is time-consuming and complicated and cannot be performed effectively without automated tools. Fully automated analysis Fully automated analysis quickly and simply assesses suspicious files. The analysis can determine potential repercussions if the malware were to infiltrate the network and then produce an easy-to-read report that provides fast answers for security teams. Fully automated analysis is the best way to process malware at scale. Manual code reversing In this stage, analysts reverse-engineer code using debuggers, disassemblers, compilers and specialized tools to decode encrypted data, determine the logic behind the malware algorithm and understand any hidden capabilities that the malware has not yet exhibited. Code reversing is a rare skill, and executing code reversals takes a great deal of time. For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. Learn More Learn about the largest online malware analysis community that is field-tested by tens of thousands of users every day. Download: Falcon Sandbox Malware Analysis Data Sheet. This is important because it provides analysts with a deeper understanding of the attack and a larger set of IOCs that can be used to better protect the organization. Learn key techniques for investigating threats, identifying IOCs, and responding to attacks effectively.","searchType":"azoresBingUnscopedSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"url":"https://inventivehq.com/knowledge-base/crowdstrike/how-to-investigate-a-security-incident-using-crowdstrike-falcon","rankScore":1,"snippet":"Investigate Security Incidents in CrowdStrike: Threat Hunting \u0026 RTR Guide | Inventive HQ Home / Knowledge Base / CrowdStrike / Investigate Security Incidents in CrowdStrike: Threat Hunting \u0026 RTR Guide CrowdStrike advanced Investigate Security Incidents in CrowdStrike: Threat Hunting \u0026 RTR Guide Investigate security incidents using CrowdStrike Falcon with step-by-step detection analysis, Real-Time Response (RTR), threat hunting, and incident response. Learn to analyze detections, hunt threats, and contain compromised endpoints. 20 min read Updated January 2026 When a security incident occurs, CrowdStrike Falcon provides detailed threat intelligence, behavioral analysis, and forensic data to help administrators investigate and respond quickly. This guide explains how to analyze security alerts, trace attack patterns, and take appropriate remediation steps using the Falcon Console. This is not a comprehensive guide Step 1: Log Into the Falcon Console Open a browser and go to: https://falcon.crowdstrike.com or https://falcon.us-2.crowdstrike.com/ (Varies by tenant). Sign in using your admin credentials. Step 2: Review Detections and Alerts View Active Detections In the left-hand menu, navigate to Activity \u003E Detections. Filter results by: Severity (Low, Medium, High, Critical) Timeframe (Last 24 hours, Last 7 days, Custom) Host Name (Investigate a specific endpoint) Analyze a Detection Alert Click on a detection to open detailed information. Review the MITRE ATT\u0026CK Tactics \u0026 Techniques used in the attack. Check the Process Tree to see how the attack progressed. Look at the Threat Graph to understand relationships between processes.\uD83D\uDCCC Example: If a suspicious PowerShell script was executed, check which parent process initiated it and what actions were performed. Step 3: Investigate the Affected Endpoint View Host Details Navigate to Hosts \u003E Host Management. Search for the affected host by Hostname or IP Address. Review: Sensor Health Status (Ensure the Falcon Sensor is running). Recent Activities (Processes, network connections, registry changes). Quarantine Status (Check if the system is isolated). Perform a Live Investigation Using Real Time Response (RTR) Click on the affected host and select Real Time Response (RTR). Run diagnostic commands to gather more details: List running processes: ps \nCopy Check network connections: netstat -ano \nCopy Review scheduled tasks: schtasks /query /fo LIST \nCopy Identify suspicious activity and determine the scope of the attack. Step 4: Contain and Remediate the Threat Option 1: Quarantine the Host In Host Management, select the affected endpoint. Click Contain Host to isolate it from the network. This prevents further spread while investigation continues. Option 2: Kill Malicious Processes In Real Time Response (RTR), run the following command (Replace with the process ID of the malicious activity):- kill  Option 3: Delete a Malicious File Run the following command in RTR: del \u0022C:\\\\path\\\\to\\\\ malware.exe\u0022  This removes the malware from the system. Step 5: Check for Additional Compromise Indicators Hunt for Related Threats Navigate to Threat Intelligence \u003E IOCs. Search for file hashes, domains, or IP addresses linked to the attack. Cross-check detections to see if other endpoints were affected. Review Falcon OverWatch Threat Reports Go to OverWatch \u003E Threat Hunting Reports. Look for campaign-based attacks and persistent threats. Take action on any additional endpoints showing suspicious activity. Step 6: Apply Prevention Measures Update Prevention Policies Go to Configuration \u003E Prevention Policies. Tighten existing rules: Increase machine learning sensitivity. Restrict PowerShell and script execution. Enable USB Device Control if applicable. Block Malicious IOCs Navigate to Threat Intelligence \u003E Indicators of Compromise (IOCs). Click Add IOC and enter known malicious file hashes, domains, or IPs. Set action to Detect \u0026 Prevent to block future attacks. Patch Vulnerabilities Use Falcon Spotlight to check for missing security patches. Apply necessary OS and software updates across all endpoints. Best Practices for Security Incident Investigation ✅ Act Quickly – Investigate high-severity detections immediately. ✅ Use Real-Time Response (RTR) – Gain deeper visibility into compromised hosts. ✅ Contain First, Investigate Second – Prevent further damage by isolating threats. ✅ Enable Threat Hunting – Use Falcon OverWatch for proactive security monitoring. Frequently Asked Questions Find answers to common questions What are the best practices for using Real-Time Response (RTR) in CrowdStrike Falcon during an active security incident? To effectively use Real-Time Response (RTR) during a security incident, ensure you have admin-level access in the Falcon Console. Start by listing running processes with \u0027ps\u0027 to identify suspicious activity, such as unusual parent-child relationships. Check network connections using \u0027netstat -ano\u0027 to detect unauthorized outbound communications, which may indicate command and control (C2) activity. Document your findings for reporting and future reference. Establish a baseline of normal activity to differentiate between false positives and real threats. If you find a malicious process, use the \u0027kill\u0027 command with the process ID (PID) to terminate it, but avoid killing system-critical processes. After isolating the affected host with the \u0027Contain Host\u0027 feature, review other endpoints for similar behaviors using Threat Intelligence IOCs. How can I effectively correlate findings from multiple endpoints during a security incident investigation in CrowdStrike Falcon? To correlate findings across multiple endpoints in CrowdStrike Falcon during a security incident, utilize the Threat Intelligence and IOC features. Start by accessing \u0027Threat Intelligence\u0027 to cross-reference IOCs, such as malicious file hashes or IP addresses, to identify affected endpoints. Then, go to \u0027Activity \u003E Detections\u0027 and filter alerts by severity and timeframe to prioritize investigations. Look for patterns, such as multiple endpoints executing the same PowerShell script, indicating a coordinated attack. Implement a real-time incident response team strategy to discuss findings, and leverage Falcon OverWatch Threat Reports for insights into ongoing campaigns. Finally, consolidate your findings into a post-incident report to enhance future detection and response strategies. What steps should I take to tighten prevention policies in CrowdStrike Falcon after a security incident has been identified? After a security incident, tighten your CrowdStrike Falcon prevention policies by following these steps: Go to \u0027Configuration \u003E Prevention Policies\u0027 and increase machine learning sensitivity to \u0027high\u0027 to enhance detection, accepting potential false positives. Restrict PowerShell and script execution by whitelisting approved scripts to reduce attack surfaces. Enable USB Device Control if removable media is unnecessary to prevent data exfiltration. In \u0027Threat Intelligence \u003E Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)\u0027, add known malicious file hashes, IPs, or domains and set their action to \u0027Detect \u0026 Prevent\u0027. Use Falcon Spotlight to identify and patch any missing updates on endpoints, addressing vulnerabilities that could be exploited in future attacks. These measures will enhance your security posture and mitigate future risks. Need help shipping something? The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red. 3 Read top-down: Parent → Child The topmost process is the root (usually explorer.exe for user-initiated or services.exe for system-initiated). Each indented level below is a child process launched by its parent. Example: Normal vs. Suspicious Process Trees Normal — User opens a Word document: explorer.exe (user desktop shell) └─ WINWORD.EXE (Microsoft Word) └─ splwow64.exe (print spooler — normal for Word) Suspicious — Word document drops a payload: explorer.exe └─ WINWORD.EXE └─ cmd.exe /c powershell -enc SQBFAFgA. ← ⚠️ Word spawning cmd/PowerShell = malicious └─ powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass ← ⚠️ Encoded command \u002B bypass └─ whoami.exe ← ⚠️ Reconnaissance └─ net.exe user /domain ← ⚠️ AD enumeration Suspicious — Lateral movement via PsExec: services.exe (system) └─ PSEXESVC.exe ← ⚠️ PsExec service installed remotely └─ cmd.exe └─ net.exe use \\DC01\\C$ /user:admin ← ⚠️ Mapping admin shares └─ vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet ← \uD83D\uDD34 RANSOMWARE PRECURSOR Red Flags to LOLBins (Living-off-the-Land Binaries) are legitimate Windows tools that attackers abuse to avoid detection. The most common ones: powershell.exe , cmd.exe , rundll32.exe , mshta.exe , certutil.exe , bitsadmin.exe , regsvr32.exe , wscript.exe , cscript.exe , msiexec.exe . These are not malicious by themselves — it\u0027s the context (parent process, command-line args, file path) that makes them suspicious. \uD83D\uDD12 Host Containment \u0026 Lift Network containment isolates a compromised endpoint from the network while keeping its connection to the CrowdStrike cloud. The host loses all network access except CrowdStrike management and any whitelisted IPs. Use this when you need to stop lateral movement or data exfiltration immediately. How to Contain a Host 1 Navigate to the host Go to Host setup and management → Host Management . Search for the hostname or IP. Click the host name to open its details. 2 Click Actions → Contain Host On the host detail page, click Actions in the top-right corner. Select \u0022Contain\u0022 from the dropdown menu. 3 Confirm containment Click Confirm . Containment should complete within seconds. The host status will change to \u0022Contained\u0022","searchType":"dataverseSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"rankScore":0.0320020467042923,"snippet":". If it shows \u0022Containment Pending,\u0022 the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by \u0022Contained\u0022 status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click \u0022Lift Containment\u0022 Click the \u0022Status: Contained\u0022 button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., \u0022Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345\u0022). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from \u0022Lift Containment Pending\u0022 to \u0022Normal\u0022 . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. \uD83C\uDFAF IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You\u0027ll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click \u0022Add IOC\u0022 Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect \u0026 Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., \u0022Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042\u0022 or \u0022CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345\u0022. 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it\u0027s already in the system before adding a duplicate. \uD83D\uDD0D Hash \u0026 IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it\u0027s malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike\u0027s intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It\u0027s the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70\u002B antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2. \uD83D\uDCBB Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Caution:","searchType":"dataverseSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"url":"https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/cybersecurity-101/malware/malware-analysis/","rankScore":1,"snippet":"Malware Analysis: Steps \u0026 Examples | CrowdStrike Cybersecurity 101: The Fundamentals of Cybersecurity \u003E Malware Explained \u003E Malware Analysis Explained Malware Analysis Explained Kurt Baker - March 04, 2025 What is malware analysis? Malware analysis is the process of understanding the behavior and purpose of a suspicious file or URL. The output of the analysis aids in the detection and mitigation of the potential threat. The key benefit of malware analysis is that it helps incident responders and security analysts: Pragmatically triage incidents by level of severity Uncover hidden indicators of compromise (IOCs) that should be blocked Improve the efficacy of IOC alerts and notifications Enrich context when threat hunting Types of malware analysis The analysis may be conducted in a manner that is static, dynamic or a hybrid of the two. Static analysis Basic static analysis does not require that the code is actually run. Instead, static analysis examines the file for signs of malicious intent. It can be useful to identify malicious infrastructure, libraries or packed files. Technical indicators are identified such as file names, hashes, strings such as IP addresses, domains, and file header data can be used to determine whether that file is malicious. In addition, tools like disassemblers and network analyzers can be used to observe the malware without actually running it in order to collect information on how the malware works. CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report AI threats have reached a critical turning point. Access the definitive look at the cyber threat landscape. Download However, since static analysis does not actually run the code, sophisticated malware can include malicious runtime behavior that can go undetected. For example, if a file generates a string that then downloads a malicious file based upon the dynamic string, it could go undetected by a basic static analysis. Enterprises have turned to dynamic analysis for a more complete understanding of the behavior of the file. Dynamic analysis Dynamic malware analysis executes suspected malicious code in a safe environment called a sandbox. This closed system enables security professionals to watch the malware in action without the risk of letting it infect their system or escape into the enterprise network. Dynamic analysis provides threat hunters and incident responders with deeper visibility, allowing them to uncover the true nature of a threat. As a secondary benefit, automated sandboxing eliminates the time it would take to reverse engineer a file to discover the malicious code. The challenge with dynamic analysis is that adversaries are smart, and they know sandboxes are out there, so they have become very good at detecting them. To deceive a sandbox, adversaries hide code inside them that may remain dormant until certain conditions are met. Only then does the code run. Hybrid analysis (includes both of the techniques above) Basic static analysis isn’t a reliable way to detect sophisticated malicious code, and sophisticated malware can sometimes hide from the presence of sandbox technology. By combining basic and dynamic analysis techniques, hybrid analysis provide security team the best of both approaches – primarily because it can detect malicious code that is trying to hide, and then can extract many more indicators of compromise (IOCs) by statically and previously unseen code. Hybrid analysis helps detect unknown threats, even those from the most sophisticated malware. For example, one of the things hybrid analysis does is apply static analysis to data generated by behavioral analysis – like when a piece of malicious code runs and generates some changes in memory. Dynamic analysis would detect that, and analysts would be alerted to circle back and perform basic static analysis on that memory dump. As a result, more IOCs would be generated and zero-day exploits would be exposed. Learn More Falcon Sandbox enables cybersecurity teams of all skill levels to increase their understanding of the threats they face and use that knowledge to defend against future attacks. Learn more about Falcon Sandbox here. Malware analysis use cases Malware detection Adversaries are employing more sophisticated techniques to avoid traditional detection mechanisms. By providing deep behavioral analysis and by identifying shared code, malicious functionality or infrastructure, threats can be more effectively detected. In addition, an output of malware analysis is the extraction of IOCs. The IOCs may then be fed into SEIMs, threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) and security orchestration tools to aid in alerting teams to related threats in the future. Threat alerts and triage Malware analysis solutions provide higher-fidelity alerts earlier in the attack life cycle. Therefore, teams can save time by prioritizing the results of these alerts over other technologies. Incident response The goal of the incident response (IR) team is to provide root cause analysis, determine impact and succeed in remediation and recovery. The malware analysis process aids in the efficiency and effectiveness of this effort. Threat hunting Malware analysis can expose behavior and artifacts that threat hunters can use to find similar activity, such as access to a particular network connection, port or domain. By searching firewall and proxy logs or SIEM data, teams can use this data to find similar threats. Malware research Academic or industry malware researchers perform malware analysis to gain an understanding of the latest techniques, exploits and tools used by adversaries. Stages of malware analysis Static properties analysis Static properties include strings embedded in the malware code, header details, hashes, metadata, embedded resources, etc. This type of data may be all that is needed to create IOCs, and they can be acquired very quickly because there is no need to run the program in order to see them. Insights gathered during the static properties analysis can indicate whether a deeper investigation using more comprehensive techniques is necessary and determine which steps should be taken next. Interactive behavior analysis Behavioral analysis is used to observe and interact with a malware sample running in a lab. Analysts seek to understand the sample’s registry, file system, process and network activities. They may also conduct memory forensics to learn how the malware uses memory. If the analysts suspect that the malware has a certain capability, they can set up a simulation to test their theory. Behavioral analysis requires a creative analyst with advanced skills. The process is time-consuming and complicated and cannot be performed effectively without automated tools. Fully automated analysis Fully automated analysis quickly and simply assesses suspicious files. The analysis can determine potential repercussions if the malware were to infiltrate the network and then produce an easy-to-read report that provides fast answers for security teams. Fully automated analysis is the best way to process malware at scale. Manual code reversing In this stage, analysts reverse-engineer code using debuggers, disassemblers, compilers and specialized tools to decode encrypted data, determine the logic behind the malware algorithm and understand any hidden capabilities that the malware has not yet exhibited. Code reversing is a rare skill, and executing code reversals takes a great deal of time. For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. If it shows \u0022Containment Pending,\u0022 the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by \u0022Contained\u0022 status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click \u0022Lift Containment\u0022 Click the \u0022Status: Contained\u0022 button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., \u0022Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345\u0022). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from \u0022Lift Containment Pending\u0022 to \u0022Normal\u0022 . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. \uD83C\uDFAF IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You\u0027ll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click \u0022Add IOC\u0022 Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect \u0026 Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., \u0022Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042\u0022 or \u0022CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345\u0022. 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it\u0027s already in the system before adding a duplicate. \uD83D\uDD0D Hash \u0026 IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it\u0027s malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike\u0027s intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It\u0027s the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70\u002B antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2. \uD83D\uDCBB Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. It can be useful to identify malicious infrastructure, libraries or packed files. Technical indicators are identified such as file names, hashes, strings such as IP addresses, domains, and file header data can be used to determine whether that file is malicious. In addition, tools like disassemblers and network analyzers can be used to observe the malware without actually running it in order to collect information on how the malware works.
