By providing deep behavioral analysis and by identifying shared code, malicious functionality or infrastructure, threats can be more effectively detected. In addition, an output of malware analysis is the extraction of IOCs. The IOCs may then be fed into SEIMs, threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) and security orchestration tools to aid in alerting teams to related threats in the future. Threat alerts and triage Malware analysis solutions provide higher-fidelity alerts earlier in the attack life cycle. Therefore, teams can save time by prioritizing the results of these alerts over other technologies. Incident response The goal of the incident response (IR) team is to provide root cause analysis, determine impact and succeed in remediation and recovery. The malware analysis process aids in the efficiency and effectiveness of this effort. Threat hunting Malware analysis can expose behavior and artifacts that threat hunters can use to find similar activity, such as access to a particular network connection, port or domain. By searching firewall and proxy logs or SIEM data, teams can use this data to find similar threats. Malware research Academic or industry malware researchers perform malware analysis to gain an understanding of the latest techniques, exploits and tools used by adversaries. Stages of malware analysis Static properties analysis Static properties include strings embedded in the malware code, header details, hashes, metadata, embedded resources, etc. This type of data may be all that is needed to create IOCs, and they can be acquired very quickly because there is no need to run the program in order to see them. Insights gathered during the static properties analysis can indicate whether a deeper investigation using more comprehensive techniques is necessary and determine which steps should be taken next. Interactive behavior analysis Behavioral analysis is used to observe and interact with a malware sample running in a lab. Analysts seek to understand the sample’s registry, file system, process and network activities. They may also conduct memory forensics to learn how the malware uses memory. If the analysts suspect that the malware has a certain capability, they can set up a simulation to test their theory. Behavioral analysis requires a creative analyst with advanced skills. The process is time-consuming and complicated and cannot be performed effectively without automated tools. Fully automated analysis Fully automated analysis quickly and simply assesses suspicious files. The analysis can determine potential repercussions if the malware were to infiltrate the network and then produce an easy-to-read report that provides fast answers for security teams. Fully automated analysis is the best way to process malware at scale. Manual code reversing In this stage, analysts reverse-engineer code using debuggers, disassemblers, compilers and specialized tools to decode encrypted data, determine the logic behind the malware algorithm and understand any hidden capabilities that the malware has not yet exhibited. Code reversing is a rare skill, and executing code reversals takes a great deal of time. For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. Learn More Learn about the largest online malware analysis community that is field-tested by tens of thousands of users every day. Download: Falcon Sandbox Malware Analysis Data Sheet. The world’s most powerful malware sandbox Security teams can use the CrowdStrike Falcon® Sandbox to understand sophisticated malware attacks and strengthen their defenses. Falcon Sandbox™ performs deep analyses of evasive and unknown threats, and enriches the results with threat intelligence. Key benefits of Falcon Sandbox Provides in-depth insight into all file, network and memory activity Offers leading anti-sandbox detection technology Generates intuitive reports with forensic data available on demand Supports the MITRE ATT\u0026CK® framework Orchestrates workflows with an extensive application programming interface (API) and pre-built integrations Detect unknown threats Falcon Sandbox extracts more IOCs than any other competing sandbox solution by using a unique hybrid analysis technology to detect unknown and zero-day exploits. All data extracted from the hybrid analysis engine is processed automatically and integrated into Falcon Sandbox reports. Falcon Sandbox has anti-evasion technology that includes state-of-the-art anti-sandbox detection. File monitoring runs in the kernel and cannot be observed by user-mode applications. There is no agent that can be easily identified by malware, and each release is continuously tested to ensure Falcon Sandbox is nearly undetectable, even by malware using the most sophisticated sandbox detection techniques. The environment can be customized by date/time, environmental variables, user behaviors and more. Identify related threats Know how to defend against an attack by understanding the adversary. Falcon Sandbox provides insights into who is behind a malware attack through the use of malware search a unique capability that determines whether a malware file is related to a larger campaign, malware family or threat actor. Falcon Sandbox will automatically search the largest malware search engine in the cybersecurity industry to find related samples and, within seconds, expand the analysis to include all files. This is important because it provides analysts with a deeper understanding of the attack and a larger set of IOCs that can be used to better protect the organization. Achieve complete visibility Uncover the full attack life cycle with in-depth insight into all file, network, memory and process activity.…","Type":"AzoresBingUnscopedSearch","Url":"https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/cybersecurity-101/malware/malware-analysis/"},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"Harris_Health_CrowdStrike_Falcon_SOP.html","SourceId":"edc7ce6f-ef82-4cef-ac7b-b7f6d349a824","Text":"or the process tree icon. The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red. 3 Read top-down: Parent → Child The topmost process is the root (usually explorer.exe for user-initiated or services.exe for system-initiated). Each indented level below is a child process launched by its parent. Example: Normal vs. Suspicious Process Trees Normal — User opens a Word document: explorer.exe (user desktop shell) └─ WINWORD.EXE (Microsoft Word) └─ splwow64.exe (print spooler — normal for Word) Suspicious — Word document drops a payload: explorer.exe └─ WINWORD.EXE └─ cmd.exe /c powershell -enc SQBFAFgA. ← ⚠️ Word spawning cmd/PowerShell = malicious └─ powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass ← ⚠️ Encoded command \u002B bypass └─ whoami.exe ← ⚠️ Reconnaissance └─ net.exe user /domain ← ⚠️ AD enumeration Suspicious — Lateral movement via PsExec: services.exe (system) └─ PSEXESVC.exe ← ⚠️ PsExec service installed remotely └─ cmd.exe └─ net.exe use \\DC01\\C$ /user:admin ← ⚠️ Mapping admin shares └─ vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet ← \uD83D\uDD34 RANSOMWARE PRECURSOR Red Flags to Look For in Process Trees \u003Ctable\u003E \u003Cthead\u003E\u003Ctr\u003E\u003Cth\u003EPattern\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EWhat It Means\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003ESeverity\u003C/th\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E\u003C/thead\u003E \u003Ctbody\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EOffice app (Word, Excel, Outlook) → cmd.exe or powershell.exe\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EMacro-based malware — the document is running commands\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHIGH\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Epowershell.exe -enc or -EncodedCommand\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EBase64-encoded PowerShell — attacker hiding their commands\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHIGH\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Ecmd.exe /c followed by whoami, ipconfig, net user\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EReconnaissance — attacker mapping the environment\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EMEDIUM\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Erundll32.exe loading an unusual DLL\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ELiving-off-the-land (LOLBin) — abusing legitimate Windows tools\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHIGH\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Emshta.exe, wscript.exe, cscript.exe from temp folder\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EScript-based malware execution\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHIGH\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Ecertutil.exe -urlcache or bitsadmin /transfer\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EDownloading payload using built-in Windows tools\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECRITICAL\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Eschtasks /create or sc create\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EEstablishing persistence — attacker wants to survive reboot\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHIGH\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Evssadmin delete shadows\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EDeleting shadow copies — imminent ransomware encryption\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECRITICAL\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EPsExec, wmic /node:, schtasks /s \u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ELateral movement — attacker spreading to other hosts\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECRITICAL\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003Emimikatz or lsass.exe access by non-system process\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECredential dumping — attacker stealing passwords/hashes\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECRITICAL\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003C/tbody\u003E \u003C/table\u003E LOLBins (Living-off-the-Land Binaries) are legitimate Windows tools that attackers abuse to avoid detection. The most common ones: powershell.exe , cmd.exe , rundll32.exe , mshta.exe , certutil.exe , bitsadmin.exe , regsvr32.exe , wscript.exe , cscript.exe , msiexec.exe . These are not malicious by themselves — it\u0027s the context (parent process, command-line args, file path) that makes them suspicious. \uD83D\uDD12 Host Containment \u0026 Lift Network containment isolates a compromised endpoint from the network while keeping its connection to the CrowdStrike cloud. The host loses all network access except CrowdStrike management and any whitelisted IPs. Use this when you need to stop lateral movement or data exfiltration immediately. How to Contain a Host 1 Navigate to the host Go to Host setup and management → Host Management . Search for the hostname or IP. Click the host name to open its details. 2 Click Actions → Contain Host On the host detail page, click Actions in the top-right corner. Select \u0022Contain\u0022 from the dropdown menu. 3 Confirm containment Click Confirm . Containment should complete within seconds. The host status will change to \u0022Contained\u0022","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""}]}},"planUsedOutputs":{},"planIdentifier":"3a6aca2e-3cdf-4ad1-8564-5a40b119cf0f","state":"completed","hasRecommendations":false,"executionTime":"00:00:00.9556851","displayedCost":7}},{"valueType":"KnowledgeTraceData","id":"6615385b292b4c92a9e8125e52057f68","type":"trace","timestamp":1781196336,"timestampMs":1781196336708,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"replyToId":"4a635e99-8eef-4966-80b3-5df973a16f87","value":{"completionState":"Answered","isKnowledgeSearched":true,"citedKnowledgeSources":[],"failedKnowledgeSourcesTypes":[]}},{"id":"bcfeb834-68a6-43c3-a640-ac3c7670abe1","type":"message","timestamp":1781196336,"timestampMs":1781196336704,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"channelId":"pva-studio","textFormat":"markdown","text":"Great question! Before diving in, a couple of clarifying points will help you triage more effectively:\n\n- **What is the CrowdStrike alert severity?** (Critical, High, Medium, Low)\n- **Is the affected system a medical device, domain controller, PHI system, or internet-facing server?**\n\nThat said, here is the full malware triage procedure per the **Harris Health CrowdStrike Falcon SOP**:\n\n---\n\n## \uD83E\uDDED Summary\nA user reporting malware issues should be triaged via the **CrowdStrike Falcon console**. Your goal is to assess severity, investigate the process tree, contain the host if necessary, and escalate appropriately.\n\n---\n\n## \uD83D\uDD22 Steps\n\n### Step 1 — Log Into the Falcon Console\nLog into [falcon.crowdstrike.com](https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using your Harris Health SSO credentials.[1]\n\nNavigate to:\n**Activity Dashboard** → Left menu → **Activity → Detections**. This is your primary alert queue. Filter by severity — **Critical/High first** — then review unassigned detections.[1]\n\n---\n\n### Step 2 — Read the Detection Alert\nReview each of the following fields on the alert card:\n\n\n| Field | What to Check |\n|---|---|\n| **Detection Name** | Is this malware, behavioral, or intel-based? |\n| **Severity** | Determines urgency and SLA |\n| **Tactic \u0026 Technique** | MITRE ATT\u0026CK — Lateral Movement or Defense Evasion = escalate quickly |\n| **Hostname** | Look up in SolarWinds for asset owner and classification |\n| **Username** | Is this an admin, clinical (Imprivata), or standard user? Admin = higher severity |\n| **File Name / Path** | \u0060C:\\Users\\...\\Downloads\u0060 is riskier than \u0060C:\\Windows\\System32\u0060 |\n| **Command Line** | Encoded PowerShell, \u0060-ExecutionPolicy Bypass\u0060, or \u0060net use \\\\\u0060 = red flags |\n| **SHA256 Hash** | Copy and look up in CrowdStrike Intelligence \u002B VirusTotal |\n| **Detection Action** | If only **Detected** (not Blocked), threat may still be active — higher urgency |\n| **Network Accesses** | Outbound to external IP on port 443/80/8080 from suspicious process = possible C2 |\n[2]\n\n\u003E ⚠️ **Overwatch Detections**: If the detection source is **Falcon Overwatch**, a human CrowdStrike threat hunter identified this activity. Treat as **HIGH confidence** and escalate to L2 immediately regardless of stated severity.[2]\n\n---\n\n### Step 3 — Check Severity SLA\n\n\n| Severity | Response | Acknowledge Within | Escalate To |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Critical** | Immediate containment. Phone L2 \u002B SOC Manager. CISO notification for ransomware/breach | \u003C 5 min | L2 \u002B SOC Mgr \u002B CISO |\n| **High** | Immediate triage. Contain if active. Always escalate to L2 | \u003C 10 min | L2 |\n| **Medium** | Triage within shift. Investigate process tree and context. Escalate if unsure | \u003C 30 min | L2 if unsure |\n| **Low** | Triage when queue permits. Check against known FP list. Document and close | \u003C 2 hours | No escalation (unless pattern of multiple Lows on one host) |\n[3]\n\n\u003E \uD83D\uDD34 **Override Rule**: If a CrowdStrike detection involves a **PHI system**, **domain controller**, **internet-facing server**, or **medical device**, add **\u002B1 severity level** to the response. A \u0022Medium\u0022 on a domain controller is treated as High.[3]\n\n---\n\n### Step 4 — Investigate the Process Tree\n\nFrom **Activity → Detections**, click the detection alert, then click **\u0022View Full Detection\u0022** or the process tree icon. The tree shows all processes in the execution chain — the detected process is highlighted in red.[2]\n\n**Key red flags to look for:**\n\n\n| Pattern | Meaning | Severity |\n|---|---|---|\n| Office app → \u0060cmd.exe\u0060 or \u0060powershell.exe\u0060 | Macro-based malware | HIGH |\n| \u0060powershell.exe -enc\u0060 or \u0060-EncodedCommand\u0060 | Attacker hiding commands | HIGH |\n| \u0060certutil.exe -urlcache\u0060 or \u0060bitsadmin /transfer\u0060 | Downloading payload with built-in tools | CRITICAL |\n| \u0060vssadmin delete shadows\u0060 | Imminent ransomware encryption | CRITICAL |\n| \u0060PsExec\u0060, \u0060wmic /node:\u0060 | Lateral movement | CRITICAL |\n| \u0060mimikatz\u0060 or \u0060lsass.exe\u0060 access | Credential dumping | CRITICAL |\n[4]\n\n---\n\n### Step 5 — Look Up File Hash / IOCs\n\n**Lookup Priority:**\n1. **CrowdStrike Intelligence first** — Go to *Threat Intelligence → Intelligence*, paste the SHA256 hash. Look for: malware family, adversary group, kill chain phase.\n2. **VirusTotal second** — Go to [virustotal.com](https://www.virustotal.com), paste the hash. Look for detection ratio and community comments.\n\n\u003E ⚠️ **OPSEC Warning**: Do **NOT** upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. Only submit **hashes (SHA256)**. If you need to sandbox a file, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2.[5]\n\n---\n\n### Step 6 — Contain the Host (If Active Threat)\n\nIf malware is actively executing, performing C2 callbacks, or showing ransomware indicators:\n\n\n1. Go to **Host Management** → search for the hostname or IP → click the host name.\n2. Click **Actions → Contain Host** → select \u0022Contain\u0022 → click **Confirm**.\n3. Refresh the host page and confirm status shows **\u0022Contained\u0022**.\n[4]The host can still communicate with the CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network.[5]\n\n\u003E \uD83C\uDFE5 **NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval.** Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead.[5]\n\n\u003E ✅ **L1 Authority**: L1 analysts **CAN** contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get **L2 approval before lifting containment** to ensure remediation is complete.[5]\n\n---\n\n## \uD83D\uDD3C Escalate If…\n- Severity is **Critical or High** → escalate to L2 immediately\n- You see **ransomware indicators** (e.g., \u0060vssadmin delete shadows\u0060) → escalate to L2 \u002B SOC Manager \u002B CISO\n- The affected system is a **domain controller, PHI system, or medical device**\n- Detection source is **Falcon Overwatch**\n- You are unsure on a **Medium** detection\n\n---\n\n## \uD83D\uDCCB Artifacts to Capture\n- Alert name, severity, timestamp\n- Hostname and IP\n- Username (admin vs. standard)\n- SHA256 hash\n- Command-line arguments\n- CrowdStrike Intelligence and VirusTotal results\n- ServiceNow ticket number\n- Containment action (if taken) with justification\n\n---\n\nDo you have the severity level or system type for this specific alert? That will help me give you more targeted guidance!\n\n[1]: cite:1 \u0022Citation-1\u0022\n[2]: cite:2 \u0022Citation-2\u0022\n[3]: cite:3 \u0022Citation-3\u0022\n[4]: cite:4 \u0022Citation-4\u0022\n[5]: cite:5 \u0022Citation-5\u0022","attachments":[],"replyToId":"4a635e99-8eef-4966-80b3-5df973a16f87","channelData":{"feedbackLoop":{"type":"default"}}},{"valueType":"DynamicPlanFinished","id":"b063d0e4-0191-4333-9e29-f490731c6c9d","type":"event","timestamp":1781196336,"timestampMs":1781196336707,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"name":"DynamicPlanFinished","channelId":"pva-studio","attachments":[],"replyToId":"4a635e99-8eef-4966-80b3-5df973a16f87","value":{"planId":"3a6aca2e-3cdf-4ad1-8564-5a40b119cf0f"}},{"valueType":"SessionInfo","id":"0","type":"trace","timestamp":1781196336,"timestampMs":1781196336707,"from":{"id":"","role":0},"value":{"startTimeUtc":"2026-06-11T16:44:33Z","endTimeUtc":"2026-06-11T16:45:36Z","type":"Engaged","outcome":"Resolved","turnCount":3,"impliedSuccess":true,"outcomeReason":"Resolved"}}]}

Use when you observe active attacker activity: mail forwarding rules, lateral movement, or data exfiltration. 4 Require MFA re-registration (recommended for all compromises) Even when MFA appears intact, require re-registration as a precaution. The attacker may have added a secondary device you haven\u0027t noticed yet. \uD83D\uDD10 MFA Method Management How to view, reset, and manage a user\u0027s MFA methods — critical during account compromise recovery. Viewing a User\u0027s MFA Methods 1 Navigate to the user Entra ID → Users → select the user → Authentication methods (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/authentication/howto-mfa-userdevicesettings) . 2 Review registered methods You\u0027ll see all registered MFA methods: phone numbers, Microsoft Authenticator app, FIDO2 keys, software OATH tokens. Check for any methods the user doesn\u0027t recognize — an attacker may have registered their own device. During Account Compromise Recovery 3 Require MFA re-registration Click \u0022Require re-register multifactor authentication\u0022 . This deletes all existing MFA methods and forces the user to set up MFA from scratch on next login. This ensures any attacker-registered methods are removed. 4 Revoke sessions Click \u0022Revoke sessions\u0022 to force re-authentication immediately. Attacker Persistence via MFA: A common attacker technique is to register their own MFA device (phone or authenticator app) on the compromised account. This lets them bypass future password resets because they can still complete MFA. Always check for unfamiliar MFA methods and require re-registration during compromise recovery. \uD83D\uDCF1 OAuth App Consent Investigation How to check for and revoke malicious OAuth app consents — a common attacker persistence technique. Why this matters: Attackers often trick users into granting OAuth permissions to malicious apps, or they add app consents during account compromise. These app permissions survive password resets and session revocations — they\u0027re one of the most persistent backdoor techniques. Checking OAuth App Consents 1 Navigate to the user\u0027s app consents Entra ID → Users → select the user → Applications → Application consents (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/enterprise-apps/manage-application-permissions) . 2 Review each consented app Look for apps the user doesn\u0027t recognize or that were consented recently (during the compromise window). Red flags: apps with names like \u0022Security Update,\u0022 \u0022Microsoft Support,\u0022 or generic names. Check the permissions granted — Mail.ReadWrite , Files.ReadWrite.All , User.ReadWrite are high-risk. 3 Revoke suspicious consents Select the suspicious app → click Revoke . This immediately removes the app\u0027s access to the user\u0027s data. L1 Checklist for OAuth: During any account compromise, always check app consents — even if you\u0027ve already reset the password and revoked sessions. If you find suspicious apps, document the app name, client ID, permissions granted, and consent timestamp in your investigation notes. \uD83D\uDCE7 Mail Forwarding \u0026 Inbox Rule Investigation Attackers commonly set up mail forwarding rules to exfiltrate email data. These rules survive password resets and session revocations — you must check and remove them explicitly during every account compromise. Why this matters: Even after you\u0027ve blocked the account, reset the password, and revoked MFA, an SMTP forwarding rule continues silently sending copies of all incoming mail to the attacker\u0027s external address. This is one of the most overlooked persistence mechanisms. Three Places to Check for Mail Forwarding 1 SMTP Forwarding (transport-level) Where: Exchange Admin Center → Recipients → Mailboxes (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/exchange/recipients-in-exchange-online/manage-user-mailboxes/email-forwarding) → select the user → Mail flow settings → Email forwarding . What to look for: Any forwarding address set, especially to an external domain. This forwards at the transport level — the user doesn\u0027t see it in their Outlook rules. Action: If forwarding to an external address is set and the user didn\u0027t configure it → remove immediately and document the destination address. 2 Inbox Rules (client-level) Where: Exchange Admin Center → select the user\u0027s mailbox → Mailbox features → Mail flow rules . Or via the Purview Audit Log (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/purview/audit-search) → search for Activity = \u0022New-InboxRule.\u0022 What to look for: Rules that forward or redirect mail to external addresses. Rules that move security notifications to deleted items or RSS feeds (hiding them from the user). Rules that mark incoming mail as read (so the user doesn\u0027t notice attacker actions). Action: Delete all unauthorized rules. Document rule names, conditions, and destination addresses. 3 Mailbox Delegate Permissions Where: Exchange Admin Center → select the user\u0027s mailbox → Delegation . Stopping Compromised Access After MFA: A Taxonomy of Containment Gaps and Controls File Size 395.03 KB Downloads 7 Files 1 Published 22 April 2026 Updated 22 April 2026 Stopping Compromised Access After MFA: A Taxonomy of Containment Gaps and Controls Sudheer Avula Independent Researcher Provo, Utah, U.S.A. sudheer.avula@gmail.com Abstract Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is widely used to strengthen account access, yet it does not by itself ensure rapid containment once authentication has succeeded. In modern authentication environments, especially enterprise and federated deployments, compromised access may persist across application sessions, identity provider sessions, access and refresh tokens, and remembered trust state. This fragmentation makes post-authentication containment a multi-layer problem rather than a single revocation event. This paper defines the post-authentication containment problem and presents a taxonomy of containment gaps and controls in MFA-protected systems. The taxonomy organizes incident classes and containment mechanisms across session, token, trust-state, and credential layers, and highlights where current controls remain partial, delayed, or dependent on product-specific integration. The paper further identifies user-driven deauthorization as an underdeveloped but potentially valuable containment capability. By clarifying this design space, the paper aims to inform future authentication architecture and support more systematic incident response. Keywords: Multi-factor authentication; post-authentication containment; session revocation; token revocation; federated identity; identity and access management; user-driven deauthorization; incident response Download Similar Downloads No related download found! ISJEM Journal Updated 22 April 2026","Type":"AzoresBingUnscopedSearch","Url":"https://isjem.com/download/stopping-compromised-access-after-mfa-a-taxonomy-of-containment-gaps-and-controls/"},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"Harris_Health_Microsoft_Entra_ID_SOP.html","SourceId":"3cc5cca6-4cd7-4569-aefa-88eb7b4dd22e","Text":"JIT admin role activations — who elevated to which admin role, when, and for how long (E5 only). L1 Action: Check during compromise investigation to see if the attacker activated any admin roles. \uD83D\uDD0D Sign-in Log Investigation Step-by-step guide to investigating suspicious sign-in activity — the most common L1 identity task. Step-by-Step: Investigating a Suspicious Sign-in 1 Open the sign-in logs and filter to the user Go to Monitoring \u0026 health → Sign-in logs . Add filter: User = the UPN of the user you\u0027re investigating. Set the date range to cover the suspicious period (usually last 7 days). 2 Check for failed sign-ins (brute force) Filter by Status = Failure . Look for: many failed attempts from the same IP, failed attempts from multiple IPs (password spray), or failures followed by a success (credential compromise). Note the source IPs and failure reasons. 3 Check for impossible travel Filter by Status = Success . Look at the Location column. Two successful sign-ins from geographically distant locations within a short time = impossible travel. Check if VPN or Cisco Secure Access was used (may explain the location difference). 4 Check MFA details Click on a specific sign-in → Authentication Details tab. Check: Was MFA required? Was it satisfied? What method was used (phone, authenticator, FIDO2)? Multiple unexpected MFA prompts = MFA fatigue attack. 5 Check Conditional Access results Click on the sign-in → Conditional Access tab. Check which policies were evaluated and their results (Success, Failure, Not Applied). If a policy that should have blocked the sign-in shows \u0022Not Applied,\u0022 there may be a CA gap. 6 Make your verdict If you find unauthorized successful sign-ins → follow the Account Compromise playbook in the Triage Decision Tree (force password reset, revoke sessions, escalate to L2). If only failed attempts with no success → monitor but no containment needed yet. Pro Tip: Copy the Request ID and Correlation ID from any suspicious sign-in. These unique identifiers let L2 and Microsoft support trace the exact authentication flow if deeper investigation is needed. \uD83D\uDD12 Account Containment Actions How to perform the four critical containment actions during an account compromise. The order depends on whether MFA has been compromised. Compliance-Reported Compromise (password known \u002B MFA changed): When Compliance notifies you that a user\u0027s password is exposed AND the attacker has changed the MFA, the attacker has full account control. Use the Block-First Order below — a standard password-first approach will fail because the attacker can re-authenticate with their own MFA device. Containment Order: MFA Compromised (Block-First) 1 BLOCK sign-in immediately Entra ID → Users → select user → Edit properties → Block sign-in = Yes (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/fundamentals/how-to-manage-user-profile-info) . This completely prevents all new authentications. Do this FIRST when the attacker has MFA control — if you only reset the password, the attacker can re-authenticate using their registered MFA device. 2 Revoke all sessions Click Revoke sessions (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/users/users-revoke-access) . Invalidates all refresh tokens. Existing access tokens expire within ~1 hour. 3 Force password reset Click Reset password (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/fundamentals/users-reset-password-azure-portal) . Set a strong temporary password. Check \u0022Require change on next sign-in.\u0022 Do NOT email the new password — the mailbox is compromised. Deliver by phone call or in person only. 4 Require MFA re-registration Go to Authentication methods → click \u0022Require re-register multifactor authentication.\u0022 This wipes ALL registered MFA methods — including whatever the attacker added. The user will need to set up MFA from scratch when the account is re-enabled. 5 Check all persistence mechanisms After containment, immediately check: OAuth app consents (see OAuth section), mail forwarding rules (see Mail Forwarding section), inbox rules , and mailbox delegates . These survive all containment actions and must be explicitly removed. Containment Order: MFA Intact (Standard) 1 Force password reset Click Reset password (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/fundamentals/users-reset-password-azure-portal) . This is safe to do first when MFA is intact — the attacker can\u0027t re-authenticate without the user\u0027s MFA device. 2 Revoke all sessions Click Revoke sessions (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/users/users-revoke-access) . 3 Block sign-in (if active attacker observed) Click Edit properties → Block sign-in = Yes (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/fundamentals/how-to-manage-user-profile-info)","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"How to Investigate an M365 Identity Compromise from Sign-in Logs to ...","SourceId":"a85c55ba-48c1-436d-8348-90dc48b39d2d","Text":"How to Investigate an M365 Identity Compromise from Sign-in Logs to Containment | Ridgeline Cyber Defence ← Back to Blog How to Investigate an M365 Identity Compromise from Sign-in Logs to Containment 3 May 2026 Incident Response \u0026 Investigation 10 min read IDENTITY COMPROMISE INVESTIGATION — THE FOUR-STEP SEQUENCE 1. HOW DID THEY GET IN? SigninLogs Attack method, source IP, CA evaluation, MFA status 2. WHAT DID THEY DO? AuditLogs \u002B OfficeActivity MFA registration, rules, OAuth grants, data access 3. HOW DO THEY PERSIST? AuditLogs \u002B ServicePrincipal New MFA methods, OAuth apps, device registration, forwarding 4. CONTAIN Revoke all persistence Sessions, MFA, OAuth, devices, mailbox rules THE MISTAKE THAT CAUSES RE-COMPROMISE Most teams do step 4 first — reset the password, revoke the session, close the ticket. The attacker re-authenticates with their own MFA method or OAuth app within hours. A user reports a suspicious email. Or Defender fires an alert for a risky sign-in. Or — more likely — someone in finance asks why the CEO sent them a wire transfer request from a Gmail address at 2am. The investigation always starts in the same place: the Entra ID sign-in logs. Not because that\u0027s where the most evidence is, but because the sign-in log answers the question that determines everything else — how did the attacker authenticate, and why did your security controls let them in? Step 1: Identify the compromise sign-in The sign-in log records every authentication attempt with the source IP, device details, application accessed, Conditional Access evaluation result, MFA status, and risk score. Your first query establishes the timeline. SigninLogs | where TimeGenerated \u003E ago(7d) | where UserPrincipalName == \u0022compromised.user@contoso.com\u0022 | where ResultType == 0 | project TimeGenerated, IPAddress, Location = tostring(LocationDetails.city), AppDisplayName, AuthenticationRequirement, ConditionalAccessStatus, DeviceDetail, RiskLevelDuringSignIn, UserAgent | sort by TimeGenerated asc You\u0027re looking for the transition point — the first successful sign-in from an unfamiliar IP or with an unfamiliar user agent. Everything before that is the legitimate user. Everything from that point forward is the investigation window. Three fields tell you the attack method immediately. If AuthenticationRequirement shows MFA was satisfied from an attacker IP, you\u0027re dealing with AiTM phishing or token theft — the attacker intercepted or replayed a valid session. If MFA was not required, Conditional Access has a gap. If MFA failed repeatedly and then succeeded, that\u0027s MFA fatigue — the user approved the push after being bombarded. The ConditionalAccessStatus field is equally important. If it shows \u0022success\u0022 for the attacker\u0027s sign-in, your Conditional Access policies evaluated the sign-in and passed it. That\u0027s a policy design failure you need to fix after containment. If it shows \u0022notApplied,\u0022 no policy matched at all — which means the application or conditions weren\u0027t covered by any policy. Step 2: Map the post-compromise activity Once you\u0027ve identified the compromise window, you need to know what the attacker did with the access. The three tables that answer this are AuditLogs, OfficeActivity, and CloudAppEvents. AuditLogs | where TimeGenerated \u003E ago(7d) | where InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName == \u0022compromised.user@contoso.com\u0022 or TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName == \u0022compromised.user@contoso.com\u0022 | project TimeGenerated, OperationName, Category, Result, Target = tostring( TargetResources[0].displayName), ModifiedProps = tostring( TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties) | sort by TimeGenerated asc This query returns every administrative action the compromised account performed — MFA method registration, device registration, application consent, group membership changes, role assignments, and password resets. Each operation has an investigation significance. AUDIT LOG OPERATIONS — INVESTIGATION SIGNIFICANCE PERSISTENCE MFA registration, device enroll, OAuth PRIVILEGE ESCALATION Role assignment, group add, PIM activation RECONNAISSANCE Directory reads, group enumeration DATA ACCESS Mail read, file download, Teams access EVASION Inbox rules, forwarding, log suppression LATERAL MOVEMENT Internal phishing, consent on behalf of Each operation tells you what the attacker was trying to achieve — and what you need to revoke. The most critical operations to look for in the first pass: \u0022User registered security info\u0022 (new MFA method — the attacker\u0027s persistence), \u0022Consent to application\u0022 (OAuth persistence), \u0022Add registered owner to device\u0022 or \u0022Register device\u0022 (device registration — bypasses Conditional Access device compliance policies), and \u0022New-InboxRule\u0022 or \u0022Set-Mailbox\u0022 with ForwardingSmtpAddress (email forwarding — the BEC setup). Step 3: Find all persistence mechanisms This is the step most teams skip. They see the compromise, reset the password, revoke sessions, and close the ticket. The attacker re-authenticates the next morning using the MFA method they registered during the 30 minutes they had access. Every persistence mechanism must be enumerated before containment. Miss one and the attacker returns. // MFA methods registered during the compromise window AuditLogs | where TimeGenerated between (datetime(2026-05-01T08:00:00Z) .. datetime(2026-05-01T12:00:00Z)) | where OperationName has_any (\u0022authentication method\u0022, \u0022registered security info\u0022, \u0022strongAuthenticationPhoneAppDetail\u0022) | where TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName == \u0022compromised.user@contoso.com\u0022 or InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName == \u0022compromised.user@contoso.com\u0022 | project TimeGenerated, OperationName, AdditionalDetails Run the same pattern for device registrations, OAuth consent grants, and mailbox rules. Each one is a separate persistence channel that survives a password reset. Persistence type Survives password reset? Survives session revoke? Where to check MFA method registration Yes Yes AuditLogs OAuth application consent Yes Yes AuditLogs \u002B ServicePrincipalSignInLogs Device registration Yes Yes AuditLogs \u002B Device management Mailbox forwarding rule Yes Yes OfficeActivity Inbox rules Yes Yes OfficeActivity Every row in that table is a \u0022yes\u0022 to both questions. That\u0027s why a password reset alone doesn\u0027t contain an identity compromise — it only removes the original access method while leaving all the persistence the attacker established during their session. Step 4: Contain — in the right order Containment for identity compromise has a specific sequence. Do it out of order and you alert the attacker before you\u0027ve removed their persistence. First, revoke all active sessions (Revoke-AzureADUserAllRefreshToken or the Entra admin portal). This kills the current session but does not prevent re-authentication. Second, remove every MFA method the attacker registered. Check the Authentication Methods blade in Entra — any method added during the compromise window gets deleted. Third, remove any devices registered during the compromise window. An attacker-registered device that shows as \u0022Entra AD registered\u0022 will satisfy Conditional Access policies requiring device registration. Fourth, revoke OAuth application consent for any applications granted during the compromise window. Check both user consent and admin consent grants. If the application has been authenticating independently (check AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs), it has its own credentials and will continue accessing data regardless of what you do to the user account. Fifth, remove mailbox rules — forwarding rules, inbox rules that move or delete messages, and any SMTP forwarding configured at the mailbox level. Sixth — now you reset the password. Not before. Every step above removes a persistence mechanism that would let the attacker re-authenticate after the reset. The query you should run right now Before you encounter your next identity compromise, run this on your own tenant to understand your baseline: AuditLogs | where TimeGenerated \u003E ago(30d) | where OperationName has_any (\u0022Consent to application\u0022, \u0022registered security info\u0022, \u0022Register device\u0022, \u0022Add registered owner\u0022) | summarize Count = count() by OperationName, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d) | render timechart This shows you the daily volume of persistence-class operations in your tenant. When a compromise happens, you\u0027ll know whether 3 new MFA registrations in a day is normal or an anomaly. Without that baseline, every investigation starts with the question \u0022is this normal?\u0022 instead of \u0022this is anomalous, here\u0027s why.\u0022 The Entra ID Security course walks through the complete identity threat landscape — every attack pattern, the defense that stops it, and the detection that catches it. The Practical Incident Response course covers the full identity compromise investigation methodology in IR8, including AiTM phishing, OAuth consent attacks, and the persistence analysis that determines containment scope. Both courses start with free modules — no account required. What to do this week Run the baseline query above on your tenant. Save the output. You\u0027ll need it when the next alert fires. Check your Conditional Access policies for the gap that appears most often in investigations: policies that require MFA but accept \u0022registered\u0022 devices instead of \u0022compliant\u0022 devices. An attacker who registers their own device satisfies \u0022registered\u0022 instantly. Verify that your IR runbook includes the six-step containment sequence — session revoke, MFA cleanup, device removal, OAuth revocation, mailbox rule removal, then password reset. If your runbook says \u0022reset password and revoke sessions,\u0022 it\u0027s missing four steps. Review the last three identity-related incidents your SOC handled. Were all persistence mechanisms checked? If the tickets were closed after a password reset, you may have unresolved persistence in your tenant right now. Next week: Endpoint security baselines — what to harden before you deploy EDR. Ridgeline Cyber Defence Written by security practitioners. Published weekly on Tuesdays.…","Type":"AzoresBingUnscopedSearch","Url":"https://training.ridgelinecyber.com/blog/m365-identity-compromise-investigation/"}]}},"planUsedOutputs":{},"planIdentifier":"e37d0c61-d944-478e-9ec2-f2da7cecfebc","state":"completed","hasRecommendations":false,"executionTime":"00:00:01.0344175"}},{"valueType":"KnowledgeTraceData","id":"e69d93a36dc4443fb64a7f8c05a99df8","type":"trace","timestamp":1778773470,"timestampMs":1778773470031,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"replyToId":"5276190d-b8bd-43e3-ab7c-d2cbbfcef9aa","value":{"completionState":"Answered","isKnowledgeSearched":true,"citedKnowledgeSources":[],"failedKnowledgeSourcesTypes":[]}},{"id":"1ce3a395-ec04-4e19-a573-6fdbaba2068a","type":"message","timestamp":1778773470,"timestampMs":1778773470027,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"channelId":"pva-studio","textFormat":"markdown","text":"## \uD83D\uDD10 Containment Sequences: MFA Compromised (Block-First) vs. MFA Intact (Standard)\n\nHere is a detailed breakdown of both sequences, the step-by-step order, and the **rationale** behind each.\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDD34 Sequence 1: MFA Compromised (Block-First)\n\n\u003E **Trigger:** Compliance notifies you that the user\u0027s password is exposed **AND** the attacker has changed or added their own MFA method.\n\nWhen the attacker has full account control (both password and MFA), a standard password-first approach will fail because the attacker can re-authenticate with their own MFA device.[1]\n\n| Step | Action | Rationale |\n|------|--------|-----------|\n| **1** | **Block sign-in immediately** (Entra ID → Users → Edit Properties → Block sign-in = Yes) | This completely prevents all new authentications. Do this FIRST when the attacker has MFA control — if you only reset the password, the attacker can re-authenticate using their registered MFA device.[1] |\n| **2** | **Revoke all sessions** | Invalidates all refresh tokens. Existing access tokens expire within ~1 hour.[1] |\n| **3** | **Force password reset** | Set a strong temporary password. Check \u0022Require change on next sign-in.\u0022 Do NOT email the new password — the mailbox is compromised. Deliver by phone call or in person only.[1] |\n| **4** | **Require MFA re-registration** | Go to Authentication methods → click \u0022Require re-register multifactor authentication.\u0022 This wipes ALL registered MFA methods — including whatever the attacker added. The user will need to set up MFA from scratch when the account is re-enabled.[1] |\n| **5** | **Check all persistence mechanisms** | After containment, immediately check: OAuth app consents, mail forwarding rules, inbox rules, and mailbox delegates. These survive all containment actions and must be explicitly removed.[1] |\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDFE1 Sequence 2: MFA Intact (Standard)\n\n\u003E **Trigger:** Password is known/compromised, but MFA has **not** been changed by the attacker.\n\n| Step | Action | Rationale |\n|------|--------|-----------|\n| **1** | **Force password reset** | This is safe to do first when MFA is intact — the attacker can\u0027t re-authenticate without the user\u0027s MFA device.[1] |\n| **2** | **Revoke all sessions** | Invalidates all active refresh tokens and kills existing sessions. |\n| **3** | **Block sign-in** *(if active attacker observed)* | Block sign-in = Yes — use when you observe active attacker activity: mail forwarding rules, lateral movement, or data exfiltration.[1] |\n| **4** | **Require MFA re-registration** *(recommended)* | Even when MFA appears intact, require re-registration as a precaution. The attacker may have added a secondary device you haven\u0027t noticed yet.[2] |\n\n---\n\n### \uD83E\uDDE0 The Core Rationale: Why Does Order Matter?\n\nThe key decision point is **who controls MFA**:\n\n| Condition | Why it changes the order |\n|-----------|--------------------------|\n| **Attacker controls MFA** | Resetting the password first gives the attacker a window to re-authenticate using *their* registered MFA device before you can block them. **Block must come first** to shut the door entirely. |\n| **MFA is intact (user controls it)** | The attacker cannot pass MFA, so resetting the password is safe to do first. A sign-in block is only needed if active lateral movement or exfiltration is observed. |\n\n\u003E ⚠️ **Escalate if:** You find evidence of admin role activation, lateral movement to other accounts, or OAuth app consents granted during the compromise window — these require **L2 escalation** and broader tenant-level investigation.\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDCCB Artifacts to Capture\n- Screenshot of the blocked account status\n- Timestamp of each containment action taken\n- List of any rogue MFA methods found and removed\n- Any suspicious OAuth apps, mail forwarding rules, or inbox rules discovered\n- Incident ticket number tied to all actions\n\n[1]: cite:1 \u0022Citation-1\u0022\n[2]: cite:2 false positive patterns in ORDR alerts. \u003Ctable\u003E\u003Cthead\u003E\u003Ctr\u003E\u003Cth\u003EFP Pattern\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003ECommon Cause\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EHow to Verify\u003C/th\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E\u003C/thead\u003E\u003Ctbody\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EAnomalous communication to new vendor cloud endpoint\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EDevice vendor updated their cloud service IP addresses (common with cloud-connected medical devices)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECheck with Clinical Engineering / vendor. If vendor confirms new endpoint → update ORDR baseline.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ENew device alert for a relocated device\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EA known device was physically moved to a different VLAN or clinic site\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECheck with Clinical Engineering. If device was relocated → update ORDR inventory and close alert.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECommunication policy violation after network change\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ENetwork infrastructure change (VLAN restructure, firewall rule update) altered the device\u0027s traffic path\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECheck with Network Ops for recent network changes. If change is authorized → update segmentation policy.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003C/tbody\u003E\u003C/table\u003E Harris Health System — InfoSec Confidential | ORDR Medical Devices SOC L1 SOP v1.0 | Last updated: March 2026 function showPanel(id) { document.querySelectorAll(\u0027.panel\u0027).forEach(p =\u003E p.classList.remove(\u0027active\u0027); document.querySelectorAll(\u0027.sidebar-btn\u0027).forEach(b =\u003E b.classList.remove(\u0027active\u0027); const panel = document.getElementById(\u0027panel-\u0027 \u002B id); if (panel) panel.classList.add(\u0027active\u0027); const btn = document.querySelector(\u0027[data-panel=\u0022\u0027 \u002B id \u002B \u0027\u0022]\u0027); if (btn) btn.classList.add(\u0027active\u0027); window.scrollTo({ top: 0, behavior: \u0027smooth\u0027 }); }","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"Overview of Device Regulation | FDA","SourceId":"a85c55ba-48c1-436d-8348-90dc48b39d2d","Text":"Overview of Device Regulation | FDA Home Medical Devices Device Advice: Comprehensive Regulatory Assistance Overview of Device Regulation Overview of Device Regulation Update: February 2, 2026 The Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR), became effective on, February 2, 2026, amending the device current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) requirements of 21 CFR part 820 and incorporating by reference the international standard specific for medical device quality management systems set by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), ISO 13485:2016 Medical devices – Quality management systems – Requirements for regulatory purposes. On February 2, 2026, the FDA stopped using the Quality System Inspection Technique (QSIT) for device inspections and began utilizing the inspection process described in the updated Inspection of Medical Device Manufacturers Compliance Program: 7382.850. After February 2, 2026, the FDA will no longer use the following documents: Inspection of Medical Device Manufacturers (7382.845) and Medical Device PMA Preapproval and PMA Postmarket Inspections (7383.001). Introduction The FDA\u0027s Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH) is responsible for regulating firms who manufacture, repackage, relabel, and/or import medical devices sold in the United States. In addition, CDRH regulates radiation-emitting electronic products (medical and non-medical) such as lasers, x-ray systems, ultrasound equipment, microwave ovens and color televisions. Radiation-emitting Electronic Products Medical devices are classified into Class I, II, and III. Regulatory control increases from Class I to Class III. The device classification regulation defines the regulatory requirements for a general device type. Most Class I devices are exempt from Premarket Notification 510 (k); most Class II devices require Premarket Notification 510 (k); and most Class III devices require Premarket Approval. A description of device classification and a link to the Product Classification Database is available at \u0022 Classification of Medical Devices.\u0022 The basic regulatory requirements that manufacturers of medical devices distributed in the U.S. must comply with are: Establishment registration, Medical Device Listing, Premarket Notification 510 (k), unless exempt, or Premarket Approval (PMA), Investigational Device Exemption (IDE) for clinical studies Quality Management System Regulation Labeling requirements, and Medical Device Reporting (MDR) Establishment Registration - 21 CFR Part 807 Manufacturers (both domestic and foreign) and initial distributors (importers) of medical devices must register their establishments with the FDA. All establishment registrations must be submitted electronically unless a waiver has been granted by the FDA. All registration information must be verified annually between October 1st and December 31st of each year. In addition to registration, foreign manufacturers must also designate a U.S. Agent. Beginning October 1, 2007, most establishments are required to pay an establishment registration fee. Establishment Registration U.S. Agents Medical Device Listing - 21 CFR Part 807 Manufacturers must list their devices with the FDA. Establishments required to list their devices include: manufacturers, contract manufacturers, contract sterilizers, repackagers and relabelers, specification developers, reprocessors single-use devices, remanufacturer manufacturers of accessories and components sold directly to the end user U.S. manufacturers of \u0022export only\u0022 devices Medical Device Listing Premarket Notification 510 (k) - 21 CFR Part 807 Subpart E If your device requires the submission of a Premarket Notification 510 (k), you cannot commercially distribute the device until you receive a letter of substantial equivalence from the FDA authorizing you to do so. A 510 (k) must demonstrate that the device is substantially equivalent to one legally in commercial distribution in the United States: (1) before May 28, 1976; or (2) to a device that has been determined by the FDA to be substantially equivalent. Premarket Notification 510 (k) On October 26, 2002 the Medical Device User Fee and Modernization Act of 2002 became law. It authorized the FDA to charge a fee for medical device Premarket Notifcation 510 (k) reviews. A small business may pay a reduced fee. The application fee applies to Traditional, Abbreviated, and Special 510 (k)s. The payment of a premarket review fee is not related in any way to the FDA\u0027s final decision on a submission. 510 (k) Review Fees Most Class I devices and some Class II devices are exempt from the Premarket Notification 510 (k) submission. A list of exempt devices is located at: 510 (k) Exempt Devices If you plan to send a 510 (k) application to the FDA for a Class I or Class II device, you may find 510 (k) review by an Accredited Persons beneficial. The FDA accredited 12 organizations to conduct a primary review of 670 types of devices. By law, the FDA must issue a final determination within 30 days after receiving a recommendation from an Accredited Person. Please note that 510 (k) review by an Accredited Person is exempt from any FDA fee; however, the third-party may charge a fee for its review. Third Party Review Premarket Approval (PMA) - 21 CFR Part 814 Product requiring PMAs are Class III devices are high risk devices that pose a significant risk of illness or injury, or devices found not substantially equivalent to Class I and II predicate through the 510 (k) process. The PMA process is more involved and includes the submission of clinical data to support claims made for the device. Premarket Approval Beginning fiscal year 2003 (October 1, 2002 through September 30, 2003), medical device user fees apply to original PMAs and certain types of PMA supplements. Small businesses are eligible for reduced or waived fees. PMA Review Fees Investigational Device Exemption (IDE) - 21 CFR Part 812 An investigational device exemption (IDE) allows the investigational device to be used in a clinical study in order to collect safety and effectiveness data required to support a Premarket Approval (PMA) application or a Premarket Notification 510 (k) submission to the FDA. Clinical studies with devices of significant risk must be approved by the FDA and by an Institutional Review Board (IRB) before the study can begin. Studies with devices of nonsignificant risk must be approved by the IRB only before the study can begin. Investigational Device Exemption Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR) - 21 CFR Part 820 The Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR) includes requirements that govern the methods used in, and the facilities and controls used for the design, manufacture, packaging, labeling, storage, installation and servicing of all finished devices intended for human use. Manufacturing facilities undergo FDA inspections to assure compliance with the QMSR. Quality Management System Regulation Labeling - 21 CFR Part 801 Labeling includes labels on the device as well as descriptive and informational literature that accompanies the device. Labeling Medical Device Reporting - 21 CFR Part 803 Incidents in which a device may have caused or contributed to a death or serious injury must be reported to the FDA under the Medical Device Reporting program. In addition, certain malfunctions must also be reported. The MDR regulation is a mechanism for the FDA and manufacturers to identify and monitor significant adverse events involving medical devices. The goals of the regulation are to detect and correct problems in a timely manner. Medical Device Reporting Additional Information Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) CDRH Freedom of Information (FOI) Reference Sheet Feedback form Was this page helpful? Yes No Device Advice. Overview of regulations for medical devices: premarket notifications (510 (k)), establishment registration, device listing, quality systems, labeling and reporting \u003Ch1\u003EORDR — SOC L1 SOP\u003C/h1\u003E Harris Health System — Security Operations Center v1.0 — Mar 2026 Sections \uD83C\uDFE0 Overview \uD83D\uDDA5️ Console Navigation \uD83D\uDD14 Alert Types \uD83D\uDEE1️ Compensating Controls \uD83D\uDEAB Common False Positives ORDR — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure Harris Health\u0027s medical device and IoMT security platform . Discovers, classifies, and monitors ~2,000\u002B medical devices across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Generates segmentation policy recommendations for Cisco FMC. Patient safety is always the top priority — never take actions that could disrupt clinical care. Console Login: ORDR Dashboard (https://ordr.net/platform/) \u003Ctable\u003E\u003Cthead\u003E\u003Ctr\u003E\u003Cth\u003ECapability\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EHarris Health Usage\u003C/th\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E\u003C/thead\u003E\u003Ctbody\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EDevice Discovery\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EPassively discovers all connected devices on medical VLANs — infusion pumps, imaging systems, patient monitors, lab equipment, HVAC, physical security.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EDevice Classification\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIdentifies device type, manufacturer, model, firmware version, clinical function, and FDA class.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EVulnerability Mapping\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EMaps known CVEs to specific device models. Enriches with device context (can it be patched? is there a vendor fix?).\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EBehavioral Monitoring\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EBaselines normal communication patterns for each device and alerts on anomalies (unexpected connections, protocol changes).\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ESegmentation Policy Generation\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EGenerates recommended network segmentation rules for Cisco FMC based on each device\u0027s required communication paths.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003C/tbody\u003E\u003C/table\u003E CRITICAL RULE — Patient Safety First: NEVER isolate, disable, power off, or modify a medical device without explicit Clinical Engineering approval. These devices may be actively involved in patient care. When in doubt, apply network-level compensating controls (Cisco FMC microsegmentation) instead of device-level actions.","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""}]}},"planUsedOutputs":{},"planIdentifier":"a7fb6fb4-0311-4d96-9950-5d5f5849d491","state":"completed","hasRecommendations":false,"executionTime":"00:00:01.1488578"}},{"valueType":"KnowledgeTraceData","id":"bcd9a8b8ea224714b6ab18abaa9e89ef","type":"trace","timestamp":1778773532,"timestampMs":1778773532940,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"replyToId":"5f720240-6707-4b5b-937f-002f3f3759ce","value":{"completionState":"Answered","isKnowledgeSearched":true,"citedKnowledgeSources":[],"failedKnowledgeSourcesTypes":[]}},{"id":"8049cbad-24f5-4f66-8944-8d53a26305cd","type":"message","timestamp":1778773532,"timestampMs":1778773532937,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"channelId":"pva-studio","textFormat":"markdown","text":"## \uD83C\uDFE5 Top Rule for Medical Devices: **Patient Safety First**\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDEA8 The #1 Critical Rule\n\n**CRITICAL RULE — Patient Safety First:**\n\u003E **NEVER isolate, disable, power off, or modify a medical device without explicit Clinical Engineering approval. These devices may be actively involved in patient care. When in doubt, apply network-level compensating controls (Cisco FMC microsegmentation) instead of device-level actions.**[1]\n\n---\n\n### \uD83E\uDDE0 Why This Rule Exists\n\nMedical devices at Harris Health — such as infusion pumps, patient monitors, imaging systems, and lab equipment — are directly tied to patient care. Harris Health\u0027s ORDR platform discovers, classifies, and monitors **2,000\u002B medical devices** across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics.[1] Any disruption to these devices, even with security intent, can have **life-safety consequences**.\n\n---\n\n### ✅ What You Should Do Instead\n\nWhen a medical device is flagged with a security concern, follow this approach:\n\n| ❌ Do NOT | ✅ Instead |\n|-----------|-----------|\n| Isolate the device | Apply **Cisco FMC microsegmentation** at the network level |\n| Disable or power off the device | Consult **Clinical Engineering** before any action |\n| Modify device settings | Document findings and escalate per SOP |\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDCDE Escalate If…\n- You need to take **any action on the device itself** → Contact **Clinical Engineering** first and obtain explicit approval.\n- You are unsure whether a device is currently in active patient use → **Assume it is** and escalate immediately.\n\n\u003E ⚠️ **Bottom line for L1 analysts:** Your primary containment tool for medical devices is the **network layer (Cisco FMC)**, not the device itself. Clinical Engineering owns the device — always loop them in.\n\n[1]: cite:1 \u0022Citation-1\u0022","attachments":[],"replyToId":"5f720240-6707-4b5b-937f-002f3f3759ce","channelData":{"feedbackLoop":{"type":"default"}}},{"valueType":"DynamicPlanFinished","id":"1ed87cae-6fad-40d2-a57d-b397c0117848","type":"event","timestamp":1778773532,"timestampMs":1778773532938,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"name":"DynamicPlanFinished","channelId":"pva-studio","attachments":[],"replyToId":"5f720240-6707-4b5b-937f-002f3f3759ce","value":{"planId":"a7fb6fb4-0311-4d96-9950-5d5f5849d491"}},{"valueType":"SessionInfo","id":"0","type":"trace","timestamp":1778773532,"timestampMs":1778773532938,"from":{"id":"","role":0},"value":{"startTimeUtc":"2026-05-14T15:43:58Z","endTimeUtc":"2026-05-14T15:45:32Z","type":"Engaged","outcome":"Resolved","turnCount":4,"impliedSuccess":true,"outcomeReason":"Resolved"}}]}

to handle and triage a malware threat incident?","searchTerms":[],"searchResults":[{"url":"https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/cybersecurity-101/malware/malware-analysis/","rankScore":1,"snippet":"Malware Analysis: Steps \u0026 Examples | CrowdStrike Cybersecurity 101: The Fundamentals of Cybersecurity \u003E Malware Explained \u003E Malware Analysis Explained Malware Analysis Explained Kurt Baker - March 04, 2025 What is malware analysis? Malware analysis is the process of understanding the behavior and purpose of a suspicious file or URL. The output of the analysis aids in the detection and mitigation of the potential threat. The key benefit of malware analysis is that it helps incident responders and security analysts: Pragmatically triage incidents by level of severity Uncover hidden indicators of compromise (IOCs) that should be blocked Improve the efficacy of IOC alerts and notifications Enrich context when threat hunting Types of malware analysis The analysis may be conducted in a manner that is static, dynamic or a hybrid of the two. Static analysis Basic static analysis does not require that the code is actually run. Instead, static analysis examines the file for signs of malicious intent. It can be useful to identify malicious infrastructure, libraries or packed files. Technical indicators are identified such as file names, hashes, strings such as IP addresses, domains, and file header data can be used to determine whether that file is malicious. In addition, tools like disassemblers and network analyzers can be used to observe the malware without actually running it in order to collect information on how the malware works. CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report AI threats have reached a critical turning point. Access the definitive look at the cyber threat landscape. Download However, since static analysis does not actually run the code, sophisticated malware can include malicious runtime behavior that can go undetected. For example, if a file generates a string that then downloads a malicious file based upon the dynamic string, it could go undetected by a basic static analysis. Enterprises have turned to dynamic analysis for a more complete understanding of the behavior of the file. Dynamic analysis Dynamic malware analysis executes suspected malicious code in a safe environment called a sandbox. This closed system enables security professionals to watch the malware in action without the risk of letting it infect their system or escape into the enterprise network. Dynamic analysis provides threat hunters and incident responders with deeper visibility, allowing them to uncover the true nature of a threat. As a secondary benefit, automated sandboxing eliminates the time it would take to reverse engineer a file to discover the malicious code. The challenge with dynamic analysis is that adversaries are smart, and they know sandboxes are out there, so they have become very good at detecting them. To deceive a sandbox, adversaries hide code inside them that may remain dormant until certain conditions are met. Only then does the code run. Hybrid analysis (includes both of the techniques above) Basic static analysis isn’t a reliable way to detect sophisticated malicious code, and sophisticated malware can sometimes hide from the presence of sandbox technology. By combining basic and dynamic analysis techniques, hybrid analysis provide security team the best of both approaches – primarily because it can detect malicious code that is trying to hide, and then can extract many more indicators of compromise (IOCs) by statically and previously unseen code. Hybrid analysis helps detect unknown threats, even those from the most sophisticated malware. For example, one of the things hybrid analysis does is apply static analysis to data generated by behavioral analysis – like when a piece of malicious code runs and generates some changes in memory. Dynamic analysis would detect that, and analysts would be alerted to circle back and perform basic static analysis on that memory dump. As a result, more IOCs would be generated and zero-day exploits would be exposed. Learn More Falcon Sandbox enables cybersecurity teams of all skill levels to increase their understanding of the threats they face and use that knowledge to defend against future attacks. Learn more about Falcon Sandbox here. Malware analysis use cases Malware detection Adversaries are employing more sophisticated techniques to avoid traditional detection mechanisms. By providing deep behavioral analysis and by identifying shared code, malicious functionality or infrastructure, threats can be more effectively detected. In addition, an output of malware analysis is the extraction of IOCs. The IOCs may then be fed into SEIMs, threat intelligence platforms (TIPs) and security orchestration tools to aid in alerting teams to related threats in the future. Threat alerts and triage Malware analysis solutions provide higher-fidelity alerts earlier in the attack life cycle. Therefore, teams can save time by prioritizing the results of these alerts over other technologies. Incident response The goal of the incident response (IR) team is to provide root cause analysis, determine impact and succeed in remediation and recovery. The malware analysis process aids in the efficiency and effectiveness of this effort. Threat hunting Malware analysis can expose behavior and artifacts that threat hunters can use to find similar activity, such as access to a particular network connection, port or domain. By searching firewall and proxy logs or SIEM data, teams can use this data to find similar threats. Malware research Academic or industry malware researchers perform malware analysis to gain an understanding of the latest techniques, exploits and tools used by adversaries. Stages of malware analysis Static properties analysis Static properties include strings embedded in the malware code, header details, hashes, metadata, embedded resources, etc. This type of data may be all that is needed to create IOCs, and they can be acquired very quickly because there is no need to run the program in order to see them. Insights gathered during the static properties analysis can indicate whether a deeper investigation using more comprehensive techniques is necessary and determine which steps should be taken next. Interactive behavior analysis Behavioral analysis is used to observe and interact with a malware sample running in a lab. Analysts seek to understand the sample’s registry, file system, process and network activities. They may also conduct memory forensics to learn how the malware uses memory. If the analysts suspect that the malware has a certain capability, they can set up a simulation to test their theory. Behavioral analysis requires a creative analyst with advanced skills. The process is time-consuming and complicated and cannot be performed effectively without automated tools. Fully automated analysis Fully automated analysis quickly and simply assesses suspicious files. The analysis can determine potential repercussions if the malware were to infiltrate the network and then produce an easy-to-read report that provides fast answers for security teams. Fully automated analysis is the best way to process malware at scale. Manual code reversing In this stage, analysts reverse-engineer code using debuggers, disassemblers, compilers and specialized tools to decode encrypted data, determine the logic behind the malware algorithm and understand any hidden capabilities that the malware has not yet exhibited. Code reversing is a rare skill, and executing code reversals takes a great deal of time. For these reasons, malware investigations often skip this step and therefore miss out on a lot of valuable insights into the nature of the malware. Learn More Learn about the largest online malware analysis community that is field-tested by tens of thousands of users every day. Download: Falcon Sandbox Malware Analysis Data Sheet. The world’s most powerful malware sandbox Security teams can use the CrowdStrike Falcon® Sandbox to understand sophisticated malware attacks and strengthen their defenses. Falcon Sandbox™ performs deep analyses of evasive and unknown threats, and enriches the results with threat intelligence. Key benefits of Falcon Sandbox Provides in-depth insight into all file, network and memory activity Offers leading anti-sandbox detection technology Generates intuitive reports with forensic data available on demand Supports the MITRE ATT\u0026CK® framework Orchestrates workflows with an extensive application programming interface (API) and pre-built integrations Detect unknown threats Falcon Sandbox extracts more IOCs than any other competing sandbox solution by using a unique hybrid analysis technology to detect unknown and zero-day exploits. All data extracted from the hybrid analysis engine is processed automatically and integrated into Falcon Sandbox reports. Falcon Sandbox has anti-evasion technology that includes state-of-the-art anti-sandbox detection. File monitoring runs in the kernel and cannot be observed by user-mode applications. There is no agent that can be easily identified by malware, and each release is continuously tested to ensure Falcon Sandbox is nearly undetectable, even by malware using the most sophisticated sandbox detection techniques. The environment can be customized by date/time, environmental variables, user behaviors and more. Identify related threats Know how to defend against an attack by understanding the adversary. Falcon Sandbox provides insights into who is behind a malware attack through the use of malware search a unique capability that determines whether a malware file is related to a larger campaign, malware family or threat actor. Falcon Sandbox will automatically search the largest malware search engine in the cybersecurity industry to find related samples and, within seconds, expand the analysis to include all files. This is important because it provides analysts with a deeper understanding of the attack and a larger set of IOCs that can be used to better protect the organization.
