If it shows \u0022Containment Pending,\u0022 the endpoint may be offline. 4 Verify containment Refresh the host page. Confirm status shows Contained . The host can still communicate with CrowdStrike cloud (so you can still run RTR commands and pull data) but cannot reach anything else on the network. Before containing a medical device: NEVER contain an endpoint on a medical device VLAN without Clinical Engineering approval. Medical devices may be actively involved in patient care. Use Cisco FMC microsegmentation as a compensating control instead. See the Medical Device playbook in the Triage Decision Tree. How to Lift Containment 1 Navigate to the contained host In Host Management , filter by \u0022Contained\u0022 status at the top. Click the host name. 2 Click \u0022Lift Containment\u0022 Click the \u0022Status: Contained\u0022 button. Add a comment explaining why containment is being lifted (e.g., \u0022Reimaged and verified clean by L2 — ticket INC0012345\u0022). Click Confirm . 3 Verify normal status Refresh the page. Status should change from \u0022Lift Containment Pending\u0022 to \u0022Normal\u0022 . The host regains full network access. L1 Authority: L1 analysts CAN contain a host without prior approval during an active threat (malware executing, C2 callback, ransomware indicators). Document your reasoning in the alert notes and ServiceNow ticket. L1 analysts should get L2 approval before lifting containment to ensure remediation is complete. \uD83C\uDFAF IOC Management Use this section to add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) in CrowdStrike Falcon. IOCs are pushed here from the TIP (Sentinel) automatically, but you may also need to add them manually from threat advisories. How to Add a Custom IOC 1 Navigate to IOC Management Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management . You\u0027ll see the list of all existing IOCs. 2 Click \u0022Add IOC\u0022 Select the IOC type: File Hash (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5), Domain , or IP Address . 3 Enter the IOC value For hashes, paste the full SHA256. For domains, enter without protocol (e.g., evil-domain.com , not https://evil-domain.com ). For IPs, enter the IPv4 address. 4 Set the action Detect — alerts only, does not block. Use when you want to monitor. Detect \u0026 Prevent — blocks execution/connection AND alerts. Use for confirmed malicious IOCs and active exploitation. 5 Set severity and description Match the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source: e.g., \u0022Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042\u0022 or \u0022CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345\u0022. 6 Set expiration Harris Health policy: no permanent IOCs . Set 30 days for actively exploited threats (renew if still active). Set 90 days for general threat intel. IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up. 7 Save and verify Click Save . Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits. Searching for existing IOCs: Use the search bar at the top of IOC Management. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it\u0027s already in the system before adding a duplicate. \uD83D\uDD0D Hash \u0026 IOC Lookup When you encounter an unknown file hash, IP, or domain during an investigation, use these tools to determine if it\u0027s malicious. Lookup Priority Order 1 CrowdStrike Intelligence (first) Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar. Why first: CrowdStrike\u0027s intel database includes healthcare-specific threat data and correlates with adversary campaigns. It\u0027s the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health. What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs. 2 VirusTotal (second) Where: virustotal.com (https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain. Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from 70\u002B antivirus engines. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides. What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious). Community comments. Behavioral analysis results if the file was sandboxed. OPSEC Warning: Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal. VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file. If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2. \uD83D\uDCBB Real-Time Response (RTR) Real-Time Response gives you a remote shell to a live endpoint. Use it for live forensics — pulling files, checking running processes, killing malicious processes. The endpoint must be online and have an active CrowdStrike sensor. Caution:","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"How to Import IOCs Into the CrowdStrike Falcon® Platform","SourceId":"a85c55ba-48c1-436d-8348-90dc48b39d2d","Text":"How to Import IOCs Into the CrowdStrike Falcon® Platform How to Consume Threat Feeds Introduction As part of the CrowdStrike API, the “Custom IOC APIs” allows you to retrieve, upload, update, search, and delete custom Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) that you want CrowdStrike to identify. With the ability to upload IOCs to the endpoints can automatically detect and prevent attacks identified by the indicators provided from a threat feed. Prerequisites To get started with the CrowdStrike API, you’ll want to first define the API client and set its scope. Refer to this guide to getting access to the CrowdStrike API for setting up a new API client key. For the new API client, make sure the scope includes read and write access for IOCs (Indicators of Compromise). As example IOCs, we will be using the test domain “evil-domain.com” and the file “this_does_nothing.exe” (this_does_nothing.exe (zipped), Source Code (zipped), which has a sha256 hash value of 4e106c973f28acfc4461caec3179319e784afa9cd939e3eda41ee7426e60989f . Beginning with the Crowdstrike API CrowdStrike leverages Swagger to provide documentation, reference information, and a simple interface to try out the API. Before accessing the Swagger UI, make sure that you’re already logged into the Falcon Console. Here’s a link to CrowdStrike’s Swagger UI. Authorize with your Client ID and Client Secret that’s associated with the IOC scope as shown in the guide to getting access to the CrowdStrike API. After you’re authorized, find the IOCs resource on the page. These are going to be the requests that we’ll demonstrate in this guide. Creating an IOC First, let’s create a couple of new IOCs. We will add an IOC for the domain “evil-domain.com” and the file hash “4e106c973f28acfc4461caec3179319e784afa9cd939e3eda41ee7426e60989f” from our sample file. Click on POST /indicators/entities/iocs/v1 to expand it. This will provide you with descriptions of the parameters and how you can use them. It also shows sample responses below as well. The information provided here is great at helping you understand how to issue the requests and is all very interesting, but we can actually take it to the next step by making a request directly from the interface with the “Try it out” button. This guides you on how to implement the CrowdStrike API and allows you to test requests directly while having the documentation readily available. Click on “Try it out”. The “Try it out” button will make the Example Value box editable. It is prepopulated with placeholder values which we will replace in just a moment. We can see that even though there are several keys that we can modify, the only required ones are type, value, and policy. We’ll use the required keys for now and just enter the necessary values that we need to create the IOCs. We can create an individual IOC or multiple IOCs in a single request, so we’re going to add both sample IOCs with our single request. You can edit your Example Values manually or just replace the existing contests with the following: [     { \u0022policy\u0022: \u0022detect\u0022, \u0022type\u0022:\u0022sha256\u0022, \u0022value\u0022:\u00224e106c973f28acfc4461caec3179319e784afa9cd939e3eda41ee7426e60989f\u0022 },     { \u0022policy\u0022: \u0022detect\u0022, \u0022type\u0022:\u0022domain\u0022, \u0022value\u0022:\u0022evil-domain.com\u0022     } ] Hit the “Execute” button at the bottom and you can see your response body below. If everything went as expected, you will receive a “200” under Code and no “errors” in the body of the response. If you receive a “401” error and see “access denied” in the body of the message, double check your authorization. Note: The actual curl command will include authorization information that is not shown here. Listing IOCs Now that we’ve created a few IOCs in the CrowdStrike Platform, let’s list them out. Click on GET /indicators/queries/iocs/v1 to expand it. Again, it’ll provide you with a description of the available parameters and how to use them. Now, click on the “Try it out” button. Something that you might notice right away is that instead of a single Example Value box, the IOC search resource provides a series of fields where you can enter values in directly. For example, you can enter “sha256” into the “types” box and then hit “Execute”. After we execute the request, it will pull up the sha256 hash of the IOC that we created earlier and list it in the details section below. CrowdStrike provides many other parameters that you can use to perform your searches. For example, you can narrow down your search to only IOCs created after a specified time or for specific hash values. Take a look at the other fields to see what else you can do. An example detection from an imported IOC To demonstrate what a detection based on your custom IOC looks like, we will use a Windows machine with CrowdStrike Falcon® installed. You can run our test tool “this_does_nothing.exe” (see beginning of article) and verify in the command window that opens, that the sha256 hash matches the IOC we uploaded. Immediately after you execute the test tool, you will see a detection in the Falcon UI. Deleting an IOC So far, we’ve created a few IOCs and searched for them. Now, let’s use the Delete request to remove IOCs that we no longer want detected. Click on DELETE /indicators/entities/iocs/v1 to expand it. Since deleting an IOC is a very straight forward process, there are only two parameters available here, just the type and value, both of which are required. Click on the “Try it out” button. The Delete resource also provides fields that you can fill in. We’ll enter the same sha256 value where the type is “sha256” and the value is “4e106c973f28acfc4461caec3179319e784afa9cd939e3eda41ee7426e60989f”. Just enter those values into the fields and hit the “Execute” button. Now let’s verify that we have deleted the file hash by executing the Search IOC request again. Expand the GET /indicators/queries/iocs/v1 again and this time, let’s leave all the fields blank. Since none of the fields are required, this will search through all the IOCs in our CrowdStrike environment. When we receive the response, we can see that the only IOC still listed is the domain. You can now delete the evil-domain.com with the delete request as well. Conclusion This guide is just the start of your journey with the CrowdStrike API. There is plenty of additional information in the CrowdStrike API Swagger UI, as well as in the Custom IOC APIs Documentation accessible through the Falcon console Docs menu. CrowdStrike Falcon — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure This is the complete L1 analyst guide for CrowdStrike Falcon, Harris Health\u0027s primary EDR platform . It covers ~13,000 IT endpoints across 2 hospitals and 32 clinics. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside Falcon (for Purview telemetry only). CrowdStrike Overwatch provides 24/7 human-led threat hunting on top of automated detections. Console Login: falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) — Use your Harris Health SSO credentials. If locked out, contact your SOC Manager. What Harris Health Uses CrowdStrike Falcon For \u003Ctable\u003E \u003Cthead\u003E\u003Ctr\u003E\u003Cth\u003EModule\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EPurpose at Harris Health\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EStatus\u003C/th\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E\u003C/thead\u003E \u003Ctbody\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Prevent\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ENext-gen antivirus — blocks known malware, exploits, and ransomware at the endpoint\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Insight XDR\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EEndpoint detection and response — behavioral analytics, process tree visibility, IOC matching\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Overwatch\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003E24/7 human-led threat hunting — proactive alerts from CrowdStrike\u0027s elite threat hunters\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive (Phase 0)\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Intelligence (CAO)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EThreat intel, dark web monitoring for Harris Health, adversary tracking, healthcare threat profiles\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive (Phase 0)\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIOC Management\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECustom indicator blocking/detection — IPs, domains, file hashes pushed from TIP or manually\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EReal-Time Response (RTR)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ERemote shell to endpoints for live forensics, file retrieval, process kill, script execution\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ENetwork Containment\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIsolate a compromised endpoint from the network while maintaining CrowdStrike cloud connectivity\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EActive\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003C/tbody\u003E \u003C/table\u003E Key Architecture Note: CrowdStrike Falcon is the primary EDR. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint runs in passive mode alongside it (for Purview DLP telemetry only, not for active protection). If you see a Defender alert, it\u0027s informational — CrowdStrike is the blocking/containment tool. A 6-9 month parallel evaluation is underway to make a data-driven decision on whether to keep both. \uD83D\uDDA5️ Console Navigation Use this section to find your way around the Falcon console. The console is accessed via falcon.crowdstrike.com (https://falcon.crowdstrike.com) using Harris Health SSO. Key Areas You\u0027ll Use Daily 1 Activity Dashboard (Home) Where: Landing page after login, or left menu → Activity → Detections What it shows: All active detections across the environment, sorted by severity and time. This is your primary alert queue for CrowdStrike alerts. L1 Action: Start here every shift. Filter by severity (Critical/High first), then review unassigned detections. 2 Host Management Where: Left menu → Host setup and management → Host Management What it shows: All enrolled endpoints — hostname, IP, OS, sensor version, containment status, last seen time. L1 Action: Search for a specific host by hostname or IP. Check if a host is online, contained, or in Reduced Functionality Mode (RFM). This is where you initiate Network Containment . 3 Threat Intelligence → IOC Management Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management What it shows: All custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the TIP automatically or manually by analysts. L1 Action: Add new IOCs from threat advisories, search for existing IOCs, check if an indicator is already blocked. 4 Threat Intelligence → Intelligence Where: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → Intelligence What it shows: CrowdStrike\u0027s threat intelligence database — adversary profiles, malware families, vulnerability intelligence, IOC reputation lookups.","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"Harris_Health_CrowdStrike_Falcon_SOP.html","SourceId":"edc7ce6f-ef82-4cef-ac7b-b7f6d349a824","Text":"Recurring FP? If you see the same false positive pattern 3\u002B times, submit a detection tuning request to your SOC Manager. They\u0027ll work with CrowdStrike to create an exclusion or adjust the detection rule so it stops firing on benign activity. \uD83D\uDCD6 Detection Names Decoder Common CrowdStrike detection names and what they mean. When you see a detection fire, use this table to quickly understand the category. \u003Ctable\u003E \u003Cthead\u003E\u003Ctr\u003E\u003Cth\u003EDetection Name\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003EWhat It Means\u003C/th\u003E\u003Cth\u003ETypical Action\u003C/th\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E\u003C/thead\u003E \u003Ctbody\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EMalware Detection\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EStatic analysis (ML or signature) identified the file as malware\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECheck if blocked. If blocked → verify quarantine, close as TP-Prevented. If not blocked → isolate host.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIntel Detection\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EA file hash, IP, or domain matched a CrowdStrike Intelligence IOC or a custom IOC you added\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHigh confidence. Check which IOC matched. If custom IOC from your TIP → verify the source advisory.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ESuspicious Process\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EBehavioral analysis flagged a process doing something unusual (but not confirmed malware)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EInvestigate the process tree. Check command-line args. May be FP (IT tool) or TP (attacker).\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EMachine Learning Detection\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECrowdStrike\u0027s ML model flagged the file based on characteristics, not a known signature\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EHigher FP rate than Intel Detection. Check file path, signer, and VirusTotal. If unsigned \u002B suspicious path → likely TP.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EOverwatch Detection\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EA CrowdStrike human threat hunter identified this activity\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EAUTO-ESCALATE to L2 — highest confidence. Do not close without L2 review.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EPrevention / Blocked\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EFalcon Prevent blocked execution of a file or process\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EVerify the block in the detection detail. If a known-bad file was blocked → TP-Prevented, close with notes.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ERansomware Detection\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EBehavioral analysis detected ransomware-like activity (mass file encryption, extension changes)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIMMEDIATE ACTION — contain host, escalate to L2 \u002B SOC Manager \u002B CISO. See Ransomware playbook.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECredential Theft\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EProcess accessed LSASS memory or performed credential dumping techniques\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003ECRITICAL — indicates mimikatz or similar. Contain host, escalate to L2.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003Ctr\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EIOA (Indicator of Attack)\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EBehavioral pattern matched a known attack technique — even if no malware file is present\u003C/td\u003E\u003Ctd\u003EInvestigate the full process tree. IOAs catch fileless attacks and LOLBin abuse. Context is everything.\u003C/td\u003E\u003C/tr\u003E \u003C/tbody\u003E \u003C/table\u003E ⚔️ MITRE ATT\u0026CK Quick Decoder CrowdStrike maps every detection to a MITRE ATT\u0026CK tactic and technique. The tactic tells you what the attacker is trying to do in the kill chain. Use this to assess how far an attack has progressed. Kill Chain Rule of Thumb: The further down this table you go, the deeper the attacker is in the environment and the more urgently you need to respond. Early-stage tactics (Reconnaissance, Initial Access) may be probing. Late-stage tactics (Lateral Movement, Exfiltration, Impact) mean the attacker has progressed significantly — escalate immediately.","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""}]},"downloaded_files":null},"planUsedOutputs":{},"planIdentifier":"7c611abf-32dc-4bc5-8440-5d320c88accb","state":"completed","hasRecommendations":false,"executionTime":"00:00:01.1017545","displayedCost":7}},{"valueType":"KnowledgeTraceData","id":"232f2823bfbb4732a187cc0ff6a53432","type":"trace","timestamp":1781196197,"timestampMs":1781196197282,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"replyToId":"0af9fa21-aa29-4c48-8274-3c46de7cddd6","value":{"completionState":"Answered","isKnowledgeSearched":true,"citedKnowledgeSources":[],"failedKnowledgeSourcesTypes":[]}},{"id":"07333a01-6500-4e66-9721-6c1d1af260cd","type":"message","timestamp":1781196197,"timestampMs":1781196197278,"from":{"id":"3f1995eb-c938-b516-e3b7-a6ba0d818fbf","role":0},"channelId":"pva-studio","textFormat":"markdown","text":"## \uD83C\uDFAF IOC Management — Deep Dive\n\n**Summary:** IOC Management in CrowdStrike Falcon is where you add, search, and manage custom Indicators of Compromise to either **detect** or **block** known-malicious file hashes, IPs, and domains across Harris Health\u0027s ~13,000 endpoints.\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDCCD Where to Find It\n\n**Navigation:** Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management\n\nThis shows all custom IOCs (IPs, domains, file hashes) that have been added — either from the **TIP (Sentinel) automatically** or **manually by analysts**.[1]\n\n---\n\n### ➕ How to Add a Custom IOC — Step by Step\n\n\n\n**Step 1 — Navigate to IOC Management**\nGo to: Left menu → Threat Intelligence → IOC Management. You\u0027ll see the list of all existing IOCs.\n\n**Step 2 — Click \u0022Add IOC\u0022**\nSelect the IOC type:\n- **File Hash** (SHA256 preferred, also SHA1 or MD5)\n- **Domain**\n- **IP Address**\n\n**Step 3 — Enter the IOC value**\n- For hashes → paste the full **SHA256**.\n- For domains → enter **without protocol** (e.g., \u0060evil-domain.com\u0060, NOT \u0060https://evil-domain.com\u0060).\n- For IPs → enter the **IPv4 address**.\n\n**Step 4 — Set the Action**\n- \uD83D\uDD0D **Detect** — alerts only, does **not block**. Use when you want to **monitor**.\n- \uD83D\uDEAB **Detect \u0026 Prevent** — blocks execution/connection **AND** alerts. Use for **confirmed malicious IOCs** and active exploitation.\n\n**Step 5 — Set Severity and Description**\nMatch the advisory severity (Critical, High, Medium, Low). In the description, include the source, e.g.:\n- \u0060\u0022Health-ISAC Advisory 2026-042\u0022\u0060\n- \u0060\u0022CISA KEV — CVE-2026-12345\u0022\u0060\n\n**Step 6 — Set Expiration**\nHarris Health policy: **no permanent IOCs**.\n- Set **30 days** for actively exploited threats (renew if still active).\n- Set **90 days** for general threat intel.\n- IOCs auto-expire and are cleaned up.\n\n**Step 7 — Save and Verify**\nClick **Save**. Verify the IOC appears in the IOC Management list. Monitor the Detection Summary for any hits.\n\n[2]\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDD0D How to Search for Existing IOCs\n\nUse the **search bar at the top of IOC Management**. Paste a hash, IP, or domain to check if it\u0027s already in the system **before adding a duplicate**.[2]\n\n\u003E ✅ **Always search before adding** — duplicate IOCs can cause confusion and clutter the list.\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDD0E How IOC Hits Appear in Detections\n\nWhen a file or connection matches one of your custom IOCs, it fires as an **\u0022Intel Detection\u0022** in the alert queue.\n\n**Intel Detection** means: a file hash, IP, or domain matched a CrowdStrike Intelligence IOC or a custom IOC you added.\n\n**Typical Action:** High confidence. Check which IOC matched. If the custom IOC came from your TIP → verify the source advisory.[3]\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDD0D Hash \u0026 IOC Lookup Priority\n\nWhen you\u0027re unsure if an IOC is malicious and need to investigate before adding it, follow this lookup priority:\n\n\n\n**1️⃣ CrowdStrike Intelligence (first)**\n- Where: Threat Intelligence → Intelligence → paste the hash, IP, or domain in the search bar.\n- Why first: CrowdStrike\u0027s intel database includes **healthcare-specific threat data** and correlates with adversary campaigns. It\u0027s the most contextually relevant source for Harris Health.\n- What to look for: Malware family, associated adversary group, kill chain phase, severity rating, and any related IOCs.\n\n**2️⃣ VirusTotal (second)**\n- Where: [virustotal.com](https://www.virustotal.com) → paste the hash, IP, or domain.\n- Why second: VirusTotal aggregates results from **70\u002B antivirus engines**. Good for confirmation and broad coverage, but lacks the adversary context that CrowdStrike provides.\n- What to look for: Detection ratio (e.g., 45/70 engines detect = confirmed malicious), community comments, and behavioral analysis results.\n\n[2]\n\n---\n\n### ⚠️ Critical OPSEC Warning\n\n**Do NOT upload actual files from Harris Health endpoints to VirusTotal.** VirusTotal is a public service — uploaded files become visible to other researchers and potentially adversaries. **Only submit hashes (SHA256) for lookup, never the actual file.** If you need to detonate a file in a sandbox, use CrowdStrike\u0027s sandbox or coordinate with L2.[2]\n\n---\n\n### \uD83D\uDCCB Quick Reference Summary\n\n| IOC Type | Format | Example |\n|---|---|---|\n| **File Hash** | SHA256 (preferred) | \u00604e106c973f28acfc...\u0060 |\n| **Domain** | No protocol prefix | \u0060evil-domain.com\u0060 |\n| **IP Address** | IPv4 | \u0060192.168.1.100\u0060 |\n\n| Action | When to Use |\n|---|---|\n| **Detect** | Monitoring / unsure / threat intel feeds |\n| **Detect \u0026 Prevent** | Confirmed malicious / active exploitation |\n\n| Expiration | When to Use |\n|---|---|\n| **30 days** | Actively exploited threats |\n| **90 days** | General threat intel |\n\n---\n\n\uD83D\uDCA1 **Need help with a specific scenario?** For example — *\u0022I have a hash from a threat advisory, how do I add it?\u0022* or *\u0022A detection fired matching an IOC, what do I do next?\u0022* — just ask and I\u0027ll walk you through it step by step!\n\n[1]: cite:1 \u0022Citation-1\u0022\n[2]: cite:2 \u0022Citation-2\u0022\n[3]: cite:3 SOPs and security procedures are available for SOC analysts?","searchTerms":[],"searchResults":[{"url":"https://msspsecurity.com/security-operations-center-workflow/","rankScore":1,"snippet":"How a Security Operations Center Workflow Really Works - MSSP Security Consulting | Product Strategy & Auditing Experts How a Security Operations Center Workflow Really Works Richard K. Stephens March 13, 2026 Inside an MSSP SOC Workflow Security operations center workflow is the structured process teams use to detect, triage, investigate, and resolve cyber threats. Most follow guidance like NIST SP 800-61, using defined escalation paths, layered analysts, and automation to keep incidents under control. In our work with MSSPs, we’ve seen how structure directly shapes outcomes. Without a clear flow, alerts stack up and analysts burn out fast. When workflows are defined, response times improve and confidence rises across the team. The difference isn’t theory, it shows up in daily operations. Keep reading to see how effective SOC workflows are built and where they often fail. Security Operations Center Workflow Highlights A clear SOC workflow lowers the mean time to respond (MTTR) by standardizing how alerts are sorted, escalated, and contained. Using tiered analyst roles spreads out the work and helps prevent burnout in a 24/7 operation. Automation and well-built playbooks can cut false positives by 40 to 60 percent when they’re set up correctly. What is a Security Operations Center Workflow? Simply put, a SOC workflow is the repeatable process a security team follows from the moment an alert pops up until the threat is gone. It uses standard operating procedures (SOPs) and clear rules for escalation. At its heart, this workflow connects three things: your people, your processes, and your technology. Logs come into a SIEM, where alerts wait in a queue. Analysts check them. Real incidents get passed up to more senior team members. Then, the response team contains the threat and helps with recovery. For teams still defining roles, understanding what a SOC actually does helps clarify how detection, escalation, and response fit together in practice. The SANS Institute has found that teams with documented runbooks respond much faster than those trying to figure it out in the moment. A good workflow makes sure you have: A clear way to classify how severe an alert is. Defined steps for when and how to escalate a problem. Measurable goals, like MTTR and dwell time. A continuous process for tuning rules to reduce false alarms. Without these guardrails, security is just putting out fires. With them, your response becomes systematic and something you can actually measure and improve. The Core Stages of a SOC Workflow Most SOC teams follow a familiar rhythm that mirrors the NIST incident response model: monitor, triage, investigate, contain, eradicate, and recover. In our consulting work with MSSPs, we see this structure everywhere, though each team shapes it based on tools and maturity. This structure becomes even clearer when mapped against a real-world inside an MSSP SOC workflow. Where monitoring, escalation, and response are tightly coordinated across multiple client environments. 1. Continuous Monitoring Everything starts with visibility. Logs from endpoints, cloud workloads, networks, and apps flow into a central platform, usually a SIEM. Analysts watch dashboards fed by firewalls, IDS tools, and identity systems. Many teams map alerts to MITRE ATT&CK so everyone speaks the same language. We’ve noticed mature SOCs focus less on volume and more on context: risk scoring, behavior analytics, and tighter correlation rules. 2. Alert Triage and Investigation Tier 1 analysts validate alerts first. In real environments, false positives still dominate, especially when rules are new. We often audit cases where tuning alone cuts noise dramatically. When an alert holds up, Tier 2 digs deeper, pivoting across endpoint telemetry, threat intel, and lateral movement clues. 3. Containment, Eradication, and Recovery Response moves fast here. Teams isolate hosts, block indicators, and lock accounts. After containment, they remove malware and fix the root cause, then restore systems carefully. 4. Post-Incident Review Strong SOCs always look back. We’ve seen MSSPs improve quickly when they track MTTD, MTTR, and refine playbooks after every incident. How Tiered Analyst Roles Structure the Workflow A tiered SOC model splits work across L1, L2, L3, and leadership so the team can scale without burning out. In many MSSP environments we’ve reviewed, problems start when roles blur and everyone handles everything. Specialization brings clarity. It also makes 24/7 coverage realistic, since each level owns a clear slice of the workflow. Clear role boundaries also reinforce core security operations center functions, ensuring monitoring, investigation, and response stay aligned even as teams grow or shift to hybrid delivery models. Tier Primary Focus Key Activities Core Metrics L1 Alert Handling Triage SIEM alerts, validate signals, escalate clean cases Alert volume, SLA adherence L2 Deep Investigation Add context, pivot across tools, initiate early containment Mean time to respond (MTTR) L3 Expert Response Forensics, malware analysis, root cause work Dwell time Manager Oversight Reporting, budgeting, compliance alignment Risk reduction trends In practice, Tier 1 removes noise so others can focus. Tier 2 connects the dots and decides if a threat is real. Tier 3 handles the rare but complex cases that need deep expertise. We often help MSSPs audit tools at this layer, since poor tooling slows investigations. The structure holds up whether the SOC is internal, outsourced, or hybrid. Which Tools Power Each Stage of the SOC Workflow? A SOC needs a stack of integrated tools: SIEM, EDR, SOAR, threat intelligence platforms, and more. A mature setup brings together endpoint and network detection tools, cloud security brokers, and identity management systems. Automation is key here. It can cut down on repetitive triage work by about 40 percent, according to reports from groups like the Ponemon Institute. As noted by the blog, “Agentic AI helps by automatically querying across available sources in parallel. That includes log data in the SIEM, endpoint activity, cloud API telemetry, identity history, and email metadata. It builds a timeline of relevant evidence, explains its reasoning, and highlights which facts increased or decreased confidence.” – VMRay A quick map of tools to stages: Workflow Stage Primary Technologies Monitoring SIEM, network detection tools, log analyzers. Triage SIEM combined with SOAR, risk-scoring systems. Investigation Endpoint detection tools, threat intel platforms. Containment Extended detection tools, DNS sinkholing. Reporting Dashboards like Kibana or Grafana. Ticketing systems (like Jira or ServiceNow) move incidents through queues, while collaboration tools (like Slack or Teams) keep communication clear. In our work at MSSP Security, we build automation into these layers to handle high-volume alerts. The point isn’t to replace people, but to free them up for the complex investigative work that really matters. Tools should enable the workflow, not become the workflow. We’ve seen this across MSSP environments of all sizes. Alert overload is the usual starting point. When dashboards flood with noise, analysts shift into reactive mode. Instead of hunting threats, they spend hours validating alerts. In weaker setups, false positives can climb past 60%, especially when detection rules aren’t tuned or reviewed often. From what we observe during audits, breakdowns tend to follow familiar patterns: Endless SIEM tuning with no clear ownership Playbooks that exist but aren’t practical to follow Escalations that rely on manual handoffs Automation that promised more than it delivered The impact shows up quickly. Burnout rises, turnover follows, and institutional knowledge disappears. New analysts inherit messy workflows and repeat the cycle. We often step in when MSSPs feel stuck between too many tools and too little consistency. What stands out is this: most teams don’t lack technology. They lack alignment. Detection engineering, vulnerability context, and workflow design need to evolve together. We’ve learned that progress usually comes from tightening processes first, then validating whether the tools truly support how the SOC operates day to day. How to Optimize a SOC Workflow for Faster Response Optimizing means working on playbooks, automation, metrics, and testing, continuously. 1. Standardize Playbooks Document specific steps for common scenarios: ransomware, phishing, zero-day exploits. Clear containment steps and escalation rules remove guesswork in a crisis. 2. Automate Repetitive Tasks Use Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platforms to automatically enrich alert data with threat intelligence or to isolate a compromised device after an analyst confirms it. This protects service-level agreements even during off-hours. 3. Measure What Matters Track the numbers that show real performance: Mean time to detect (MTTD) Mean time to respond (MTTR) The percentage of false positives reduced Adherence to breach notification timelines Dashboards for leadership should translate these tech metrics into business risk terms. 4. Test Constantly Run purple team exercises, simulation drills, and tabletop discussions. These tests find weak spots in your detection logic before a real attacker does. We treat optimization as ongoing engineering. Our experience shows that combining smart orchestration with skilled analysts consistently lowers response times and leads to better audit results. What an AI-Driven SOC Workflow Looks Like Modern SOCs are starting to lean on AI, but not in the way marketing suggests. In the environments we review, AI mostly helps sort and prioritize. For organizations operating a Security Operations Center (SOC), aligning with NIST security best practices strengthens detection, response, compliance, and overall cyber resilience. The NIST CSF is built around five core functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. Together, these functions serve as a practical roadmap for building, auditing, and improving your SOC. In this guide, we explain: NIST CSF SOC implementation A complete security operations center audit checklist How to perform a SOC gap assessment Whether managed SOC services are right for your organization What Is a Security Operations Center (SOC)? A Security Operations Center (SOC) is a centralized cybersecurity function responsible for monitoring, detecting, analyzing, and responding to security incidents in real time. A modern SOC combines: Skilled security personnel Documented processes Advanced security technologies Continuous threat intelligence Rather than acting as a passive monitoring team, a NIST-aligned SOC proactively manages cyber risk and strengthens organizational resilience. Most SOCs rely on: Security Information and Event Managemen t (SIEM) systems Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS) Endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools Vulnerability scanning platforms When aligned with NIST security best practices, a SOC becomes a strategic risk management function instead of just a reactive support team. SOC Roles and Responsibilities An effective NIST security operations center includes clearly defined roles across multiple tiers: Tier 1 – Security Analyst Monitors alerts Performs initial incident triage Escalates verified threats Tier 2 – Incident Responder Investigates confirmed threats Executes containment procedures Supports remediation and recovery Tier 3 – Threat Hunter Proactively searches for hidden or advanced threats Leverages threat intelligence Conducts advanced SOC gap assessments SOC Manager Oversees operations Reports findings to executive leadership and the CISO Ensures NIST CSF best practices are implemented Security Engineer / Architect Designs and maintains security infrastructure Supports NIST CSF SOC implementation Optimizes detection and response tools Request a Free Consultation Security Operations Center Audit Checklist (NIST CSF-Based) The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) provides the most widely accepted foundation for a security operations center audit checklist. Each of the five core functions serves as a structured audit category: Identify Establish visibility into assets, risks, and business context. Checklist: Maintain an updated asset inventory Conduct formal risk assessments Define organizational risk tolerance Map regulatory requirements (HIPAA, PCI DSS, etc.) Implement supply chain risk management controls Protect Implement safeguards to prevent or limit cyber incidents. Checklist: Enforce strong identity and access management (IAM) Conduct ongoing security awareness training Protect sensitive data with encryption and access controls Apply secure configurations and patch management Maintain documented protection policies Detect Enable continuous monitoring and early threat identification. Checklist: Deploy continuous monitoring capabilities Define anomaly detection processes Integrate real-time threat intelligence feeds Test detection controls regularly Respond Define structured incident response procedures. Checklist: Maintain a documented incident response plan Establish stakeholder communication protocols Conduct root cause analysis Perform mitigation activities Continuously improve response capabilities Recover Restore systems and services after a cybersecurity event. Checklist: Test recovery plans regularly Restore systems according to defined recovery objectives Update recovery strategies based on lessons learned Communicate recovery status internally and externally Conducting a SOC Gap Assessment A SOC gap assessment compares your current security operations against NIST CSF best practices to identify weaknesses. Gap assessments help organizations: Measure SOC maturity Identify missing controls Prioritize remediation efforts Strengthen compliance readiness Reduce regulatory risk While internal teams can perform assessments, third-party cybersecurity specialists often provide deeper insight and unbiased evaluation. Regular audits and gap assessments ensure that your NIST security operations center operates effectively and continuously improves. SOC Tools and Technologies Effective NIST security implementation depends on modern tooling and proactive testing. Common SOC technologies include: SIEM platforms Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) Vulnerability management tools Threat intelligence platforms Best practices also include: Regular penetration testing Continuous vulnerability scanning Tabletop incident response exercises Continuous control validation Proactive testing reduces the likelihood of successful cyberattacks and strengthens your detection capabilities. Managed SOC Services vs. In-House SOC Building and maintaining an internal SOC requires: 24/7 staffing Advanced expertise Ongoing tool investment Continuous training Because of these costs, many organizations turn to managed SOC services to enhance efficiency and strengthen NIST CSF SOC implementation. A Managed Security Services Provider (MSSP) can provide: Real-time threat monitoring Incident response and recovery Threat and vulnerability management Compliance alignment Continuous SOC gap assessments Outsourcing can accelerate your adoption of NIST security best practices while reducing internal operational strain. Building a NIST-Aligned Security Operations Center A Security Operations Center is your organization’s frontline defense against cyber threats. Whether you operate an internal SOC or leverage managed SOC services, aligning with NIST security best practices ensures your organization can: Detect threats earlier Respond faster Minimize damage Recover efficiently Strengthen regulatory compliance Regular SOC audits, structured gap assessments, and strategic NIST CSF implementation are essential for maintaining a mature and resilient cybersecurity posture. RSI Security provides comprehensive managed SOC services designed to help organizations implement, audit, and optimize their NIST security operations center strategy. Contact RSI Security today to strengthen your cybersecurity framework. Simply put, a SOC workflow is the repeatable process a security team follows from the moment an alert pops up until the threat is gone. It uses standard operating procedures (SOPs) and clear rules for escalation. At its heart, this workflow connects three things: your people, your processes, and your technology. Logs come into a SIEM, where alerts wait in a queue. Analysts check them. Real incidents get passed up to more senior team members. Then, the response team contains the threat and helps with recovery. A good workflow makes sure you have: A clear way to classify how severe an alert is. Defined steps for when and how to escalate a problem. Measurable goals, like MTTR and dwell time. A continuous process for tuning rules to reduce false alarms. Without these guardrails, security is just putting out fires. Primary identity investigation tool.</td></tr> <tr><td>Audit Logs</td><td>All changes to users, groups, apps, roles — password changes, MFA method registration, group membership changes.</td></tr> <tr><td>Conditional Access</td><td>Policy-based access control: require MFA, require compliant device, block risky sign-ins. E5 users get full CA; F3 users get basic (known gap).</td></tr> <tr><td>MFA Management</td><td>View, reset, and revoke MFA methods for users. Critical during account compromise response.</td></tr> <tr><td>Session Revocation</td><td>Revoke all active sessions and tokens for a user — kills attacker access immediately.</td></tr> <tr><td>Password Reset</td><td>Admin-initiated password resets. Used during compromise containment. SSPR being deployed for self-service.</td></tr> <tr><td>PIM (E5 only)</td><td>Privileged Identity Management — just-in-time admin role elevation with approval workflows.</td></tr> </tbody></table> F3 Licensing Gap: The 10,100 F3/FLW users have only basic Conditional Access (no full CA policies). This means MFA enforcement is limited for clinical/frontline staff. Imprivata handles clinical authentication separately via tap-and-go. 🖥️ Console Navigation Key areas of the Entra admin center you'll use for identity investigations. 1 Sign-in Logs Where: Entra ID → Monitoring & health → Sign-in logs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/monitoring-health/howto-access-activity-logs) What it shows: Every authentication attempt — user, app, IP, location, device, MFA method, CA policy result, success/failure. L1 Action: This is your primary tool for investigating brute force, impossible travel, MFA bypass, and account compromise. Filter by user UPN, date range, status (Success/Failure), and IP address. 2 Audit Logs Where: Entra ID → Monitoring & health → Audit logs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/monitoring-health/concept-audit-logs) What it shows: All directory changes — password resets, MFA method changes, group membership changes, app consent grants, role assignments. L1 Action: Check during account compromise for unauthorized changes. Filter by Service = "Self-service Password Management" for password events. 3 User Management Where: Entra ID → Users → All users → search for the user What it shows: User profile, group memberships, assigned roles, registered devices, authentication methods, sign-in activity. L1 Action: This is where you reset passwords , revoke sessions , block sign-in , and manage MFA methods . 4 Conditional Access Where: Entra ID → Protection → Conditional Access (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/conditional-access/overview) What it shows: All CA policies, their conditions, and grant/session controls. L1 Action: Check what CA policies applied to a sign-in (visible in sign-in log detail → Conditional Access tab). Useful for understanding why a sign-in succeeded or was blocked. 5 PIM (Privileged Identity Management) Where: Entra ID → Identity Governance → PIM (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/id-governance/privileged-identity-management/pim-how-to-use-audit-log) What it shows:","searchType":"dataverseSearch","searchSubType":"none","isFileSnippet":false},{"rankScore":0.014925372786819935,"snippet":"Microsoft Purview — SOC L1 SOP | Harris Health <img alt="Harris Health" src="data:image/png;base64,"/> <h1>Microsoft Purview — SOC L1 SOP</h1> Harris Health System — Security Operations Center v1.0 — Mar 2026 Sections 🏠 Overview 🖥️ Console Navigation 🏷️ Sensitivity Labels 🔔 DLP Alert Triage 💾 USB / Endpoint DLP 🚫 Common False Positives Microsoft Purview — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure Harris Health's data protection and DLP platform (Phase 1-2 deployment). If an attack exceeds this capacity, upstream ISP mitigation may be needed — escalate to L2 + Network Ops immediately.","Type":"DataverseSearch","Url":""},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"How a Security Operations Center Workflow Really Works","SourceId":"a85c55ba-48c1-436d-8348-90dc48b39d2d","Text":"How a Security Operations Center Workflow Really Works - MSSP Security Consulting | Product Strategy & Auditing Experts How a Security Operations Center Workflow Really Works Richard K. Stephens March 13, 2026 Inside an MSSP SOC Workflow Security operations center workflow is the structured process teams use to detect, triage, investigate, and resolve cyber threats. Most follow guidance like NIST SP 800-61, using defined escalation paths, layered analysts, and automation to keep incidents under control. In our work with MSSPs, we’ve seen how structure directly shapes outcomes. Without a clear flow, alerts stack up and analysts burn out fast. When workflows are defined, response times improve and confidence rises across the team. The difference isn’t theory, it shows up in daily operations. Keep reading to see how effective SOC workflows are built and where they often fail. Security Operations Center Workflow Highlights A clear SOC workflow lowers the mean time to respond (MTTR) by standardizing how alerts are sorted, escalated, and contained. Using tiered analyst roles spreads out the work and helps prevent burnout in a 24/7 operation. Automation and well-built playbooks can cut false positives by 40 to 60 percent when they’re set up correctly. What is a Security Operations Center Workflow? Simply put, a SOC workflow is the repeatable process a security team follows from the moment an alert pops up until the threat is gone. It uses standard operating procedures (SOPs) and clear rules for escalation. At its heart, this workflow connects three things: your people, your processes, and your technology. Logs come into a SIEM, where alerts wait in a queue. Analysts check them. Real incidents get passed up to more senior team members. Then, the response team contains the threat and helps with recovery. For teams still defining roles, understanding what a SOC actually does helps clarify how detection, escalation, and response fit together in practice. The SANS Institute has found that teams with documented runbooks respond much faster than those trying to figure it out in the moment. A good workflow makes sure you have: A clear way to classify how severe an alert is. Defined steps for when and how to escalate a problem. Measurable goals, like MTTR and dwell time. A continuous process for tuning rules to reduce false alarms. Without these guardrails, security is just putting out fires. With them, your response becomes systematic and something you can actually measure and improve. The Core Stages of a SOC Workflow Most SOC teams follow a familiar rhythm that mirrors the NIST incident response model: monitor, triage, investigate, contain, eradicate, and recover. In our consulting work with MSSPs, we see this structure everywhere, though each team shapes it based on tools and maturity. This structure becomes even clearer when mapped against a real-world inside an MSSP SOC workflow. Where monitoring, escalation, and response are tightly coordinated across multiple client environments. 1. Continuous Monitoring Everything starts with visibility. Logs from endpoints, cloud workloads, networks, and apps flow into a central platform, usually a SIEM. Analysts watch dashboards fed by firewalls, IDS tools, and identity systems. Many teams map alerts to MITRE ATT&CK so everyone speaks the same language. We’ve noticed mature SOCs focus less on volume and more on context: risk scoring, behavior analytics, and tighter correlation rules. 2. Alert Triage and Investigation Tier 1 analysts validate alerts first. In real environments, false positives still dominate, especially when rules are new. We often audit cases where tuning alone cuts noise dramatically. When an alert holds up, Tier 2 digs deeper, pivoting across endpoint telemetry, threat intel, and lateral movement clues. 3. Containment, Eradication, and Recovery Response moves fast here. Teams isolate hosts, block indicators, and lock accounts. After containment, they remove malware and fix the root cause, then restore systems carefully. 4. Post-Incident Review Strong SOCs always look back. We’ve seen MSSPs improve quickly when they track MTTD, MTTR, and refine playbooks after every incident. How Tiered Analyst Roles Structure the Workflow A tiered SOC model splits work across L1, L2, L3, and leadership so the team can scale without burning out. In many MSSP environments we’ve reviewed, problems start when roles blur and everyone handles everything. Specialization brings clarity. It also makes 24/7 coverage realistic, since each level owns a clear slice of the workflow. Clear role boundaries also reinforce core security operations center functions, ensuring monitoring, investigation, and response stay aligned even as teams grow or shift to hybrid delivery models. Tier Primary Focus Key Activities Core Metrics L1 Alert Handling Triage SIEM alerts, validate signals, escalate clean cases Alert volume, SLA adherence L2 Deep Investigation Add context, pivot across tools, initiate early containment Mean time to respond (MTTR) L3 Expert Response Forensics, malware analysis, root cause work Dwell time Manager Oversight Reporting, budgeting, compliance alignment Risk reduction trends In practice, Tier 1 removes noise so others can focus. Tier 2 connects the dots and decides if a threat is real. Tier 3 handles the rare but complex cases that need deep expertise. We often help MSSPs audit tools at this layer, since poor tooling slows investigations. The structure holds up whether the SOC is internal, outsourced, or hybrid. Which Tools Power Each Stage of the SOC Workflow? A SOC needs a stack of integrated tools: SIEM, EDR, SOAR, threat intelligence platforms, and more. A mature setup brings together endpoint and network detection tools, cloud security brokers, and identity management systems. Automation is key here. It can cut down on repetitive triage work by about 40 percent, according to reports from groups like the Ponemon Institute. As noted by the blog, “Agentic AI helps by automatically querying across available sources in parallel. That includes log data in the SIEM, endpoint activity, cloud API telemetry, identity history, and email metadata. It builds a timeline of relevant evidence, explains its reasoning, and highlights which facts increased or decreased confidence.” – VMRay A quick map of tools to stages: Workflow Stage Primary Technologies Monitoring SIEM, network detection tools, log analyzers. Triage SIEM combined with SOAR, risk-scoring systems. Investigation Endpoint detection tools, threat intel platforms. Containment Extended detection tools, DNS sinkholing. Reporting Dashboards like Kibana or Grafana. Ticketing systems (like Jira or ServiceNow) move incidents through queues, while collaboration tools (like Slack or Teams) keep communication clear. In our work at MSSP Security, we build automation into these layers to handle high-volume alerts. The point isn’t to replace people, but to free them up for the complex investigative work that really matters. Tools should enable the workflow, not become the workflow. Why Do SOC Workflows Break Down in Practice? 🔴 Modern SOC Analyst Workflows Simply Cyber - Gerald Auger, PhD Watch on Credits: Simply Cyber – Gerald Auger, PhD Even well-designed SOC workflows can fall apart under pressure. We’ve seen this across MSSP environments of all sizes. Alert overload is the usual starting point. When dashboards flood with noise, analysts shift into reactive mode. Instead of hunting threats, they spend hours validating alerts. In weaker setups, false positives can climb past 60%, especially when detection rules aren’t tuned or reviewed often. From what we observe during audits, breakdowns tend to follow familiar patterns: Endless SIEM tuning with no clear ownership Playbooks that exist but aren’t practical to follow Escalations that rely on manual handoffs Automation that promised more than it delivered The impact shows up quickly. Burnout rises, turnover follows, and institutional knowledge disappears. New analysts inherit messy workflows and repeat the cycle. We often step in when MSSPs feel stuck between too many tools and too little consistency. What stands out is this: most teams don’t lack technology. They lack alignment. Detection engineering, vulnerability context, and workflow design need to evolve together. We’ve learned that progress usually comes from tightening processes first, then validating whether the tools truly support how the SOC operates day to day. How to Optimize a SOC Workflow for Faster Response Optimizing means working on playbooks, automation, metrics, and testing, continuously. 1. Standardize Playbooks Document specific steps for common scenarios: ransomware, phishing, zero-day exploits. Clear containment steps and escalation rules remove guesswork in a crisis. 2. Automate Repetitive Tasks Use Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platforms to automatically enrich alert data with threat intelligence or to isolate a compromised device after an analyst confirms it. This protects service-level agreements even during off-hours. 3. Measure What Matters Track the numbers that show real performance: Mean time to detect (MTTD) Mean time to respond (MTTR) The percentage of false positives reduced Adherence to breach notification timelines Dashboards for leadership should translate these tech metrics into business risk terms. 4. Test Constantly Run purple team exercises, simulation drills, and tabletop discussions. These tests find weak spots in your detection logic before a real attacker does. We treat optimization as ongoing engineering. Our experience shows that combining smart orchestration with skilled analysts consistently lowers response times and leads to better audit results. What an AI-Driven SOC Workflow Looks Like Modern SOCs are starting to lean on AI, but not in the way marketing suggests. In the environments we review, AI mostly helps sort and prioritize. User behavior analytics and risk scoring push the most suspicious alerts to the top, so analysts spend less time filtering noise.…","Type":"AzoresBingUnscopedSearch","Url":"https://msspsecurity.com/security-operations-center-workflow/"},{"FileType":"","IconUrl":"","Name":"Harris_Health_Microsoft_Entra_ID_SOP.html","SourceId":"3cc5cca6-4cd7-4569-aefa-88eb7b4dd22e","Text":"Microsoft Entra ID — SOC L1 SOP | Harris Health <img alt="Harris Health" src="data:image/png;base64,"/> <h1>Microsoft Entra ID — SOC L1 SOP</h1> Harris Health System — Security Operations Center v1.0 — Mar 2026 Sections 🏠 Overview 🖥️ Console Navigation 🔍 Sign-in Investigation 🔒 Account Containment 🔐 MFA Management 📱 OAuth App Consents 📧 Mail Forwarding Rules 🚫 Common False Positives 📋 Quick Reference Microsoft Entra ID — SOC L1 Standard Operating Procedure Harris Health's identity and access management platform for all 13,100 users (3,000 E5 + 10,100 F3). ---

## 🛡️ Harris Health SOC L1 SOP Assistant — What I Can Do

I'm your **step-by-step, SOP-driven guide** for the Harris Health Security Operations Center. Here's a taste of the real knowledge I have loaded:

---

### 📚 SOPs I Know Cold

| Platform | What I Can Help With |
|---|---|
| 🌐 **Radware DefensePro** | Harris Health's on-premises DDoS protection platform, capable of 50 Gbps attack mitigation. I can walk you through alert triage, console navigation, and escalation for SYN floods, UDP floods, ICMP floods, and DNS amplification attacks.[1] |
| 🔐 **Microsoft Entra ID** | Harris Health's identity and access management platform for all 13,100 users. I cover sign-in investigations, account containment, MFA management, session revocation, OAuth app consents, and mail forwarding rule reviews.[2] |
| 🏷️ **Microsoft Purview** | Harris Health's data protection and DLP platform. I cover sensitivity label triage, email DLP alerts, SharePoint/OneDrive/Teams DLP, endpoint DLP (USB/print), and PHI breach escalation procedures.[3] |

---

### ⚡ Real Things I Can Walk You Through Right Now

- 🚨 **"We're getting a DDoS alert — what do I do?"**
- 👤 **"A user's account looks compromised — how do I contain it?"**
- 📧 **"Purview flagged a PHI email going externally — is this a breach?"**
- 🔒 **"How do I revoke a user's sessions and reset their MFA?"**
- 📋 **"What do I need to escalate to L2?"**

---

### 🧠 How I Work
- I give you **numbered steps** you can follow in real time
- I tell you **what success looks like**
- I tell you **when to stop and escalate**
- I tell you **what evidence to capture**

---

So... what alert just landed in your queue? 🚀
